title: The Stranger
rt_critics_consensus: A masterful blend of genre elements, The Stranger brings a tautly told mystery to life with simmering tension and a pair of outstanding lead performances.

title: Suspiria
rt_critics_consensus: Suspiria attacks heady themes with garish vigor, offering a viewing experience that's daringly confrontational - and definitely not for everyone.

title: Pledge
rt_critics_consensus: Pledge leaves a certain amount of potential untapped in its extreme take on collegiate hazing, but the end result is still well-executed enough to be worth a watch.

title: The Dead Don't Die
rt_critics_consensus: The Dead Don't Die dabbles with tones and themes to varying degrees of success, but sharp wit and a strong cast make this a zom-com with enough brains to consume.

title: The Retreat
rt_critics_consensus: It wields its horror elements more effectively than its social themes, but The Retreat is a wilderness slasher that gore-hungry viewers should savor.

title: Hell Hole
rt_critics_consensus: Hell Hole pokes much needed life into an aging genre, going back to basics and delivering the gory goods.

title: I See You
rt_critics_consensus: I See You gets tripped up on its own narrative contortions, but a solid cast and an effective blend of scares and suspense make this slow-building mystery worth watching.

title: Fright Night
rt_critics_consensus: It may not have been necessary to remake the 1985 cult classic, but the new Fright Night benefits from terrific performances by Colin Farrell and David Tennant -- and it's smart, funny, and stylishly gory to boot.

title: Knocking
rt_critics_consensus: A slow-burning thriller that teeters between reality and delusion, Knocking views social issues through a blurred lens streaked with horror.

title: Incarnate
rt_critics_consensus: Incarnate can't be accused of lack of ideas -- if only any of them made sense or coalesced in any meaningful or scary way.

title: THE BRIDE!
rt_critics_consensus: Concocted with all the restraint of a mad scientist's experiment, THE BRIDE! lurches in so many different creative directions that the overall effect is both sloppy and inspired.

title: Do You See What I See
The Visit
rt_critics_consensus: The Visit provides horror fans with a satisfying blend of thrills and laughs -- and also signals a welcome return to form for writer-director M. Night Shyamalan.

title: Sea Fever
rt_critics_consensus: If Sea Fever never quite heats up as much as it could, it remains an engrossing, well-acted sci-fi thriller with effective horror elements.

title: Monday
rt_critics_consensus: Monday has attractive leads and a beautifully filmed setting, but fatal character flaws undermine the impact of this turbulent romance.

title: Clock
rt_critics_consensus: While Clock may strain some viewers' suspension of disbelief, it's a mostly effective attempt to view reproductive pressures through a chilling horror lens.

title: Tarot
rt_critics_consensus: Reshuffling well-worn tropes to dismaying effect, Tarot's fate is decidedly dour.

title: Cobweb
rt_critics_consensus: This itsy bitsy horror film creeps and crawls.

title: Abandoned
rt_critics_consensus: Horror fans seeking effective frights will almost certainly feel Abandoned by this well-cast but deeply disappointing haunted house story.

title: The Awakening
rt_critics_consensus: The Awakening never quite quickens the pulse the way it should, yet it remains a well-acted and handsomely assembled example of an old-fashioned supernatural thriller.

title: The Blob
rt_critics_consensus: The Blob can't replicate the B-movie charms of the original, though its fast pace and gory thrills pack enough of a punch to make it a worthwhile update.

title: The Witch Who Came From the Sea
The Sacrifice
rt_critics_consensus: Formally impressive, visually accomplished, and narratively rewarding, The Sacrifice places a fittingly solid capstone on a brilliant filmmaking career.

title: Dracula
rt_critics_consensus: Radu Jude's Dracula puts its subject and audience to the test in this absurd experiment in creativity that just might suck the life out of everyone for better or for worse.

title: Monster
rt_critics_consensus: Monster would have benefited from a less heavy-handed approach, but Kelvin Harrison Jr.'s performance gives this timely drama emotional heft.

title: Becky
rt_critics_consensus: Becky isn't quite able to sustain enough intensity to fully take advantage of its premise, though it serves up entertainingly nasty thrills for genre fans.

title: The Plague
rt_critics_consensus: Steeped in disquieting mood without ever treading water, The Plague takes common adolescent anxieties and elevates them into an unforgettable chiller.

title: Extinction
rt_critics_consensus: Extinction has a few intriguing ideas, but they -- and some game performances from its talented stars -- are lost in the movie's muddled plot and frustrating pacing.

title: Unhinged
rt_critics_consensus: Russell Crowe makes for a compulsively watchable villain, but Unhinged lacks enough intelligence or depth to get sufficient mileage out of its pulpy premise.

title: Host
rt_critics_consensus: Lean, suspenseful, and scary, Host uses its timely premise to deliver a nastily effective treat for horror enthusiasts.

title: The Beast
rt_critics_consensus: Unwieldy but rewarding, The Beast uses its sci-fi conceit to explore intriguing themes in largely satisfying fashion.

title: The House of the Devil
rt_critics_consensus: Though its underlying themes are familiar, House of the Devil effectively sheds the loud and gory cliches of contemporary horror to deliver a tense, slowly building throwback to the fright flicks of decades past.

title: Pearl
rt_critics_consensus: Pearl finds Ti West squeezing fresh gore out of the world he created with X -- and once again benefiting from a brilliant Mia Goth performance.

title: The Owners
rt_critics_consensus: The Owners will prove excessively mean-spirited and over the top for some, but should be just pulpy enough for genre enthusiasts up for some home invasion thrills.

title: Death Note
rt_critics_consensus: Death Note benefits from director Adam Wingard's distinctive eye and a talented cast, but they aren't enough to overcome a fatally overcrowded canvas.

title: Thriller
rt_critics_consensus: Thriller has an appealing cast and a fresh perspective on its genre, but those elements aren't enough to outweigh a bland and predictable story.

title: House of Darkness
rt_critics_consensus: House of Darkness finds writer-director Neil LaBute just as acerbic as ever, although he's made more compelling statements on sexual mores.

title: Intruders
rt_critics_consensus: Intruders has a fantastic first act but then settles into a bland plot and scare tactics that aren't all too scary.

title: A Christmas Carol
rt_critics_consensus: This radical retelling of Charles Dickens' classic parable struggles to justify its oppressive tone and edgy flourishes, although Guy Pearce is suitably haunting as the haunted Ebenezer Scrooge.

title: Spree
rt_critics_consensus: Joe Keery's magnetic screen presence can't disguise Spree's shallow critique of social media culture -- although that lack of depth may be precisely the point.

title: Jimmy Tupper vs. the Goatman of Bowie
Kiss of the Damned
rt_critics_consensus: This one bites in a mostly good way: Kiss of the Damned is an erotic gorefest reminiscent of gaudy '70s horror flicks, presented in plain packaging and not meant for direct sunlight.

title: Scarlet
rt_critics_consensus: Scarlet is a narratively ambitious take on Hamlet that delivers awe-inspiring fantasy, wondrous animation, and an unforgettable atmosphere.

title: Mary
rt_critics_consensus: Misguided from stem to stern, Mary wastes the talents of an outstanding cast -- and makes a soggy mess of its supernatural horror story.

title: Hannah
rt_critics_consensus: Hannah rests almost completely on Charlotte Rampling's lead performance -- and her outstanding work proves more than capable of bearing the weight.

title: Chabelo and Pepito vs. The Monsters
The Power
rt_critics_consensus: A supernatural horror story grounded in real-world trauma, The Power marks writer-director Corinna Faith as an emerging talent to watch.

title: Halloween
rt_critics_consensus: Rob Zombie doesn't bring many new ideas to the table in Halloween, making it another bloody disappointment for fans of the franchise.

title: Sinister
rt_critics_consensus: Its plot hinges on typically implausible horror-movie behavior and recycles countless genre cliches, but Sinister delivers a surprising number of fresh, diabolical twists.

title: The Feast
rt_critics_consensus: Traditional horror may not be on the menu, but for fans of the gruesomely disquieting, The Feast more than lives up to its name.

title: Halloween
rt_critics_consensus: Halloween largely wipes the slate clean after decades of disappointing sequels, ignoring increasingly elaborate mythology in favor of basic - yet still effective - ingredients.

title: The Rental
rt_critics_consensus: Some tricky genre juggling makes The Rental a bit of a fixer-upper, but effective chills and a solid cast make this a fine destination for horror fans.

title: Fury: The Tales of Ronan Pierce
Honeymoon
rt_critics_consensus: Smart, stylish, and nail-bitingly tense, Honeymoon packs more slow-building horror than many bigger-budget productions.

title: Sweeney Todd: The Demon Barber of Fleet Street
rt_critics_consensus: Full of pith and Grand Guignol grossness, this macabre musical is perfectly helmed and highly entertaining. Tim Burton masterfully stages the musical in a way that will make you think he has done this many times before.

title: Hush
rt_critics_consensus: Hush navigates the bloody waters of home invasion thrillers and incisive slashers for a contemporary horror puree.

title: Still Life
rt_critics_consensus: Still Life moves with a quiet grace that some viewers may mistake for mannered passivity, but rewards patience with genuine poignancy.

title: Speak No Evil
rt_critics_consensus: Harnessing sick suspense from the glimmer in James McAvoy's eye, Speak No Evil is the rare remake that hushes up concerns of "been there, done that."

title: The Call
rt_critics_consensus: Some horror fans and '80s enthusiasts may answer The Call, although a lack of genuine scares and an overload of genre tropes will leave most struggling to maintain the connection.

title: Black Dog
rt_critics_consensus: Guan Hu delivers a poetic and redemptive journey through China with Black Dog, reminding us that the best of friends often has four legs.

title: Love Hurts
rt_critics_consensus: Ouchie.

title: War of the Worlds - The True Story
One of Us
rt_critics_consensus: Harrowing and heartbreaking, One of Us offers an intimate, revealing glimpse inside a notoriously private community and those who would dare defy it.

title: Mayhem
rt_critics_consensus: Mayhem delivers stylish violence by the bloody bucketful -- and grounds all the titular chaos in sharp humor and surprisingly effective real-world economic angst.

title: FeardotCom
rt_critics_consensus: As frustrating as a 404 error, Fear Dot Com is a stylish, incoherent, and often nasty mess with few scares.

title: Cargo
rt_critics_consensus: Cargo takes a refreshingly character-driven approach to the zombie genre that's further distinguished by its Australian setting and Martin Freeman's terrific lead performance.

title: Deep Red
rt_critics_consensus: The kinetic camerawork and brutal over-the-top gore that made Dario Argento famous is on full display, but the addition of a compelling, complex story makes Deep Red a masterpiece.

title: Undead
rt_critics_consensus: This low-budget homage to the zombie genre borrows heavily from superior predecessors and revels in a pile of its own campiness -- neither original nor watchable enough to entertain.

title: The Teacher
rt_critics_consensus: A political drama that hits a raw emotional nerve thanks in part to Saleh Bakri's terrific lead performance, The Teacher marks an auspicious feature-length debut by writer-director Farah Nabulsi.

title: Army Of The Dead
rt_critics_consensus: An ambitious, over-the-top zombie heist mashup, Army of the Dead brings Zack Snyder back to his genre roots with a suitably gory splash.

title: Cold Storage
rt_critics_consensus: A smart but familiar B-movie throwback, Cold Storage delivers campy performances, gooey splatter, and wry sci-fi wit with infectious enthusiasm.

title: Prometheus
rt_critics_consensus: Ridley Scott's ambitious quasi-prequel to Alien may not answer all of its big questions, but it's redeemed by its haunting visual grandeur and compelling performances -- particularly Michael Fassbender as a fastidious android.

title: The Fog
rt_critics_consensus: A well-crafted return to horror for genre giant John Carpenter, The Fog rolls in and wraps viewers in suitably slow-building chills.

title: Spiral
rt_critics_consensus: Spiral explores the tension between otherness and conformity with a well-acted horror story that chills even if it doesn't outright terrify.

title: I Know What You Did Last Summer
rt_critics_consensus: Faithfully harkening back to the original for better or worse, this I Know What You Did Last Summer reboot serves up plenty of nostalgia without finding a compelling hook of its own.

title: Cinderella
rt_critics_consensus: This singalong-worthy Cinderella sprinkles some modern fairy dust on the oft-told tale, but flat performances and clunky dialogue make watching often feel like a chore.

title: The Reckoning
rt_critics_consensus: Thematically and visually unpleasant, The Reckoning wallows in the same violent misogyny it purports to condemn.

title: Tales of Halloween
rt_critics_consensus: Tales of Halloween boasts a number of fun scares and is overall more consistent than many horror anthology films, even if it isn't quite as dark or nasty as the classics of the genre.

title: The Offering
rt_critics_consensus: Within the outline of its fairly standard story, The Offering puts a unique -- and often genuinely scary -- spin on demonic possession horror tropes.

title: Black Christmas
rt_critics_consensus: The rare slasher with enough intelligence to wind up the tension between bloody outbursts, Black Christmas offers fiendishly enjoyable holiday viewing for genre fans.

title: Master
rt_critics_consensus: It can be didactic rather than truly suspenseful, but Master is an impressively well-crafted horror outing with a lot on its mind.

title: The Snowman
rt_critics_consensus: A mystery that feels as mashed together and perishable as its title, The Snowman squanders its bestselling source material as well as a top-notch ensemble cast.

title: Harpoon
rt_critics_consensus: A B-movie with an A-level commitment to entertain, Harpoon should hit the target with horror fans in the mood for gory, darkly humorous antics on the open water.

title: The Masque of the Red Death
Night of the Living Dead
rt_critics_consensus: Night of the Living Dead fails to bring much new to the original's narrative tentpoles, but its sleek action and amplified gore make it a worthwhile horror showcase.

title: Dreamland
rt_critics_consensus: Dreamland's frustratingly diffuse narrative approach is consistently offset by typically strong work from Margot Robbie in a central role.

title: Black Swan
rt_critics_consensus: Bracingly intense, passionate, and wildly melodramatic, Black Swan glides on Darren Aronofsky's bold direction -- and a bravura performance from Natalie Portman.

title: Berserk: The Golden Age Arc I - The Egg of the King
Companion
rt_critics_consensus: A fiendishly clever contraption that doesn't rest on the laurels of its twists, Companion thrillingly puts the demented into domestic bliss.

title: The Wicker Man
rt_critics_consensus: Puzzlingly misguided, Neil LaBute's update The Wicker Man struggles against unintentional comedy and fails.

title: Agnes
rt_critics_consensus: It may not deliver as many straight-up scares as genre fans expect, but Agnes takes a gratifyingly unusual approach to well-worn territory.

title: The Return
rt_critics_consensus: Unoriginality may be an acceptable risk in the Horror genre, but boredom is never okay.

title: Anaconda
rt_critics_consensus: The premise might be ripe for a raucous action-comedy, but this meta reboot of Anaconda can't detach its jaws wide enough to swallow so many conflicting tones.

title: Max
rt_critics_consensus: Max has good intentions and tries to hearken back to classic family-friendly features, but its disjointed, manipulative plot overwhelms the efforts of its talented human and canine stars.

title: Bad Hair
rt_critics_consensus: Bad Hair's unwieldy ambitions are easy to respect -- even if the film's tonal jumble and uneven execution are impossible to ignore.

title: Crawl
rt_critics_consensus: An action-packed creature feature that's fast, terrifying, and benefits greatly from a completely game Kaya Scodelario, Crawl is a fun throw-back with just enough self-awareness to work.

title: The Outsider
rt_critics_consensus: The Outsider may satisfy Western fans, but despite a winning performance from Country music legend Trace Adkins it's too predictable to really leave a mark.

title: Mother, May I Sleep With Danger?
Leatherface
rt_critics_consensus: Leatherface may wear the skin of a Texas Chainsaw Massacre prequel, but it proves gutless as an origin story and finds little invention in the horror tropes it's cannibalizing.

title: Absolution
rt_critics_consensus: Liam Neeson's gruff performance redeems Absolution somewhat, this thriller's sluggish pacing may not fully earn audiences' forgiveness.

title: Mandy
rt_critics_consensus: Mandy's gonzo violence is fueled by a gripping performance by Nicolas Cage -- and anchored with palpable emotion conveyed between his volcanic outbursts.

title: The Thing
rt_critics_consensus: It serves the bare serviceable minimum for a horror flick, but The Thing is all boo-scares and a slave to the far superior John Carpenter version.

title: The Hunting Ground
rt_critics_consensus: The Hunting Ground isn't director Kirby Dick's strongest work as a filmmaker, but the movie's powerful message more than trumps any technical weaknesses.

title: The Hunt
rt_critics_consensus: Anchored by Mads Mikkelsen's sympathetic performance, The Hunt asks difficult questions with the courage to pursue answers head on.

title: Oxygen
rt_critics_consensus: Oxygen gets the white-knuckle maximum out of its claustrophobic setting, with director Alexandre Aja and star Mélanie Laurent making this a must-watch thriller for sci-fi fans.

title: The Vatican Tapes
rt_critics_consensus: A loud, rote exorcism thriller that presents nothing new to an already overpopulated subgenre.

title: Countdown
rt_critics_consensus: Countdown may offer a few fleeting jolts for horror fans with barren queues, but it lacks enough wit or creativity to leave a lasting impression.

title: Lee Cronin's The Mummy
rt_critics_consensus: Director Lee Cronin's take on The Mummy injects some juicy gore and personal stakes into the classic horror setup, but the scares in this gross-out extravaganza get entombed by a padded running time.

title: Evil Dead
rt_critics_consensus: It may lack the absurd humor that underlined the original, but the new-look Evil Dead compensates with brutal terror, gory scares, and gleefully bloody violence.

title: The Prodigy
rt_critics_consensus: The Prodigy doesn't take the bad seed genre to any truly new places, but for horror fans in search of an evil child to fear, it might still be worth a watch.

title: The Furies
rt_critics_consensus: The Furies makes up for its lack of originality with girl-power gore wielded by a ferocious Airlie Dodds.

title: Immaculate
rt_critics_consensus: Immaculate in conception if not always in execution, this religiously themed horror outing is saved by a divine performance from Sydney Sweeney.

title: Abigail
rt_critics_consensus: Carrying off well-worn vampire tropes with a balletic flourish, Abigail dances around the familiarity of its premise with a game cast and slick style.

title: Long Night in a Dead City
Watcher
rt_critics_consensus: Although its story may lack surprises, Watcher benefits from director Chloe Okuno's chilling grip on the material -- and Maika Monroe's terrific work in the leading role.

title: Resurrection
rt_critics_consensus: Pushing against the boundaries of storytelling with overwhelming style, Bi Gan's Resurrection is equal parts inscrutable and invigoratingly awake to the endless possibilities of cinema.

title: Secret Obsession
rt_critics_consensus: While it may offer some thrills and unintentional laughs, Secret Obsession is mostly a formulaic and dumb thriller.

title: Don't Let Go
rt_critics_consensus: While admirably intelligent and well-cast, Don't Let Go suffers from a formulaic approach compounded by its uneven writing and an awkward tonal balance.

title: Till Death
rt_critics_consensus: Elevated by S.K. Dale's inventive direction and Megan Fox's committed performance, Till Death will part the viewer with all but the edge of their seat.

title: Drop
rt_critics_consensus: A Hitchcockian thriller that cleverly utilizes modern technology for its twists and turns, Drop delivers on its pulpy premise while providing a welcome showcase for leading lady Meghann Fahy.

title: Good Boy
rt_critics_consensus: Good Boy is a visually striking, emotionally devastating horror film that eschews genre conventions to deliver a uniquely haunting and conceptually ambitious experience.

title: Monster
rt_critics_consensus: Gently devastating in its compassion, Monster is a masterpiece of shifting perspectives that surprises to the end.

title: Final Cut
rt_critics_consensus: Although it'll be more satisfying to viewers who haven't already seen the original, Final Cut offers playfully entertaining meta commentary on the art of the remake.

title: Frankenstein
rt_critics_consensus: Finding the humanity in one of cinema's most iconic monsters, Guillermo Del Toro's Frankenstein is a lavish epic that gets its most invigorating volts from Jacob Elordi's standout performance.

title: The Gates of Hell II: Dead Awakening
Paranormal Activity 4
rt_critics_consensus: While it does manage to wring a few more screams out of the franchise's surprisingly durable premise, Paranormal Activity 4 provides fans of the series with dismayingly diminishing returns.

title: The Silence
rt_critics_consensus: The Silence has nothing new to say with a derivative premise and placid pacing -- even a wasted Stanley Tucci is unable to elevate the stodgy material a decibel above dreadful.

title: Together
rt_critics_consensus: Given an extra sinew of authenticity by the metatextual casting of Alison Brie and Dave Franco at the top of their game, Together is a body horror that's as emotionally sticky as it is memorably gnarly.

title: Kill List
rt_critics_consensus: Kill List is an expertly executed slow-burn crime thriller that thrives on tension before morphing into visceral horror.

title: The Beast Within
rt_critics_consensus: Somewhere inside The Beast Within lies a clever riff on the werewolf genre, but it struggles to claw through a lack of incident and specificity.

title: Cell
rt_critics_consensus: Shoddily crafted and devoid of suspense, Cell squanders a capable cast and Stephen King's once-prescient source material on a bland rehash of zombie cliches.

title: Don't Move
rt_critics_consensus: Don't Move gets a reasonable amount of mileage out of its self-imposed limitations, making for a slight but effective thriller that'll get the blood pumping.

title: Caveat
rt_critics_consensus: An effective spine-tingler despite clear budget constraints, Caveat suggests a deliciously dark filmmaking future for writer-director Damian Mc Carthy.

title: Coyotes
rt_critics_consensus: Not exactly the alpha of the pack, what Coyotes lacks in bark it makes up for in its unique blend of comedic thrills.

title: Hamlet
rt_critics_consensus: Fueled by Riz Ahmed's electrifying turn, this gritty, streamlined Hamlet boldly reimagines the classic tragedy with ferocious emotion and intent.

title: It's My Party and I'll Die if I Want To
Silent Night
rt_critics_consensus: Silent Night reaffirms that an action movie doesn't need much dialogue if the set pieces are solid enough -- and that even second-tier John Woo can be worth the price of admission.

title: Here Alone
rt_critics_consensus: Here Alone may not suffer from an overabundance of narrative substance, but it offers enough post-apocalyptic action to satisfy.

title: I Spit on Your Grave
rt_critics_consensus: Well-shot exploitation that has less purpose and utility than in 1978.

title: Naruto Shippuden the Movie: Blood Prison
Cry Wolf
rt_critics_consensus: Dull and derivative, Cry_Wolf is a subpar slasher that even ardent fans of the genre may find difficult to enjoy.

title: Goosebumps
rt_critics_consensus: Goosebumps boasts more than enough of its spooky source material's kid-friendly charm to make up for some slightly scattershot humor and a hurried pace.

title: Creep
rt_critics_consensus: A smart, oddball take on found-footage horror, Creep is clever and well-acted enough to keep viewers on the edges of their seats.

title: The Babysitter
rt_critics_consensus: Enjoyable if not particularly original, The Babysitter makes the most of its familiar genre ingredients with energetic direction and a killer cast.

title: At the End of the Tunnel
Tragedy Girls
rt_critics_consensus: Tragedy Girls injects familiar teen tropes with just enough up-to-the-minute commentary -- and pitch-black humor -- to work as an irreverently entertaining diversion.

title: Stage Fright
rt_critics_consensus: There might be a good movie to be made from mashing up the slasher and musical genres; unfortunately, Stage Fright isn't quite it.

title: Nocturne
rt_critics_consensus: Nocturne's thought-provoking themes find themselves at odds with its genre ingredients, resulting in a mild blend that isn't quite pulpy enough.

title: Lucky
rt_critics_consensus: A rich blend of thrilling horror and sharp social commentary, Lucky acts as a bloody good calling card for director Natasha Kermani and writer-star Brea Grant.

title: Limbo
rt_critics_consensus: Limbo probes Aboriginal injustices with a patient eye, using the scars of history as the backdrop and basis for a powerfully minimalist mystery.

title: The House That Jack Built
rt_critics_consensus: The House That Jack Built presents writer-director Lars von Trier at his most proudly uncompromising: hard to ignore, and for many viewers, just as difficult to digest.

title: Dreams
rt_critics_consensus: Anchored by intense performances and charged intimacy, Dreams is a chilly yet compelling two-hander whose messy imperfections ultimately deepen its quietly tragic impact.

title: Shadow
rt_critics_consensus: Beautifully filmed and inventively choreographed, Shadow is a thrilling and visually sumptuous wuxia epic that finds director Zhang Yimou near peak form.

title: Hellboy
rt_critics_consensus: Bereft of the imaginative flair that made earlier Hellboys so enjoyable, this soulless reboot suggests Dante may have left a tenth circle out of his Inferno.

title: The Watchers
rt_critics_consensus: Heavy on eerie mood but undone by an obtuse script, The Watchers can only hold the gaze for so long before tedium sets in.

title: La llorona
rt_critics_consensus: La Llorona puts a fresh spin on the familiar legend by blending the supernatural and the political to resolutely chilling effect.

title: Blackout
rt_critics_consensus: Werewolf horror with some extra socially conscious bite, Blackout reaffirms Larry Fessenden as a smart genre filmmaker who prioritizes character-building over kills.

title: Child's Play
rt_critics_consensus: Child's Play updates an '80s horror icon for the Internet of Things era, with predictably gruesome -- and generally entertaining -- results.

title: The Mummy
rt_critics_consensus: Lacking the campy fun of the franchise's most recent entries and failing to deliver many monster-movie thrills, The Mummy suggests a speedy unraveling for the Dark Universe.

title: Salem's Lot
rt_critics_consensus: An old-fashioned spookfest, this Salem's Lot won't be the definitive adaptation of Stephen King's famed vampire tale but it makes for a passable reintroduction to a new generation.

title: Heretic
rt_critics_consensus: Hugh Grant has infectious fun playing against type in Heretic, a religious horror that preaches the gospel of cerebral chills over cheap shocks.

title: It
rt_critics_consensus: Well-acted and fiendishly frightening with an emotionally affecting story at its core, It amplifies the horror in Stephen King's classic story without losing touch with its heart.

title: Ride With the Devil
rt_critics_consensus: A well-acted, eccentric Civil War film, Ang Lee's Ride With the Devil is often more visually striking than it is emotionally engaging.

title: Deliver Us From Evil
rt_critics_consensus: Director Scott Derrickson continues to have a reliably firm grasp on creepy atmosphere, but Deliver Us from Evil's lack of original scares is reflected in its shopworn title.

title: Berberian Sound Studio
rt_critics_consensus: Its reach may exceed its grasp, but with Berberian Sound Studio, director Peter Strickland assembles a suitably twisted, creepy tribute to the Italian Giallo horror movies of the '70s that benefits from a strong central performance by Toby Jones.

title: Ma
rt_critics_consensus: Octavia Spencer's performance overpowers many of Ma's flaws, but uneven pacing and a labored story keep this thriller from fully realizing its unhinged potential.

title: Black Christmas
rt_critics_consensus: Better than the 2006 remake yet not as sharp as the original, this Black Christmas stabs at timely feminist themes but mostly hits on familiar pulp.

title: Into the Deep
rt_critics_consensus: Into the Deep drowns its few intriguing ideas in melodramatic acting and flimsy effects, resulting in a shallow, often laughable thriller.

title: The Sadness
rt_critics_consensus: The Sadness lives up to its title with an unstintingly grim -- and overall effective -- slice of dystopian horror.

title: Infested
rt_critics_consensus: A chillingly effective creature feature with more on its mind than simple creepy-crawlies, Infested draws viewers into its web with stylish efficiency.

title: mother!
rt_critics_consensus: There's no denying that mother! is the thought-provoking product of a singularly ambitious artistic vision, though it may be too unwieldy for mainstream tastes.

title: Us
rt_critics_consensus: With Jordan Peele's second inventive, ambitious horror film, we have seen how to beat the sophomore jinx, and it is Us.

title: Nostalgia
rt_critics_consensus: Walking the tightrope between gangster drama and character study, Nostalgia takes us through the criminal underbelly of Naples in search of a past that may be better left behind.

title: The Invisible Man
rt_critics_consensus: Smart, well-acted, and above all scary, The Invisible Man proves that sometimes, the classic source material for a fresh reboot can be hiding in plain sight.

title: Ghost Stories
rt_critics_consensus: Ghost Stories offers a well-crafted, skillfully told horror anthology that cleverly toys with genre tropes while adding a few devilishly frightful twists.

title: Finders Keepers: The Root of All Evil
The Blazing World
rt_critics_consensus: The Blazing World's scattershot script isn't always able to support writer-director-star Carlson Young's ambitions, but its arresting visuals hold the attention.

title: Smile
rt_critics_consensus: Deeply creepy visuals and a standout Sosie Bacon further elevate Smile's unsettling exploration of trauma, adding up to the rare feature that satisfyingly expands on a short.

title: Evil Eye
rt_critics_consensus: With Evil Eye, directors Elan and Rajeev Dassani spy a premise that has no shortage of potential, but despite the efforts of a game cast, it remains tantalizingly unfulfilled.

title: Ever After
rt_critics_consensus: Smart, stylish, and unpredictable, Ever After puts a refreshing spin on post-apocalyptic zombie horror.

title: Gabrielle
rt_critics_consensus: Bolstered by strong, natural performances, Gabrielle's sincere look at differently abled young adults nimbly walks the line between sweet and cloying.

title: Cure
rt_critics_consensus: Mesmerizing and psychologically intriguing.

title: Keeper
rt_critics_consensus: More a collection of unsettling moments than an altogether cohesive story, Keeper is too half-baked to have much staying power despite a committed turn from Tatiana Maslany.

title: The Mummy
rt_critics_consensus: Relying more on mood and atmosphere than the thrills typical of modern horror fare, Universal's The Mummy sets a masterful template for mummy-themed films to follow.

title: The Lovers
rt_critics_consensus: With appealing leads and a narrative approach that offers a fresh perspective on familiar themes, The Lovers tells a quietly absorbing story with unexpected emotional resonance.

title: Stoker
rt_critics_consensus: Its script doesn't quite carry the dramatic heft of his earlier work, but Park Chan-wook's Stoker showcases his eye for sumptuous imagery and his affection for dark, atmospheric narratives populated by mysterious characters.

title: Killing Ground
rt_critics_consensus: Killing Ground unnerves and compels in equal measure with a grimly intense story that may be too disturbing for some but delivers a white-knuckle experience for fans of brutally realistic thrillers.

title: Sick
rt_critics_consensus: Smart, self-aware, and all too timely, this slasher co-written by Kevin Williamson is Sick in all the best ways.

title: Witch Hunt
rt_critics_consensus: Witch Hunt's smart social commentary is undercut by its lack of original storytelling ideas, but this supernatural horror outing has just enough bite to recommend for teens.

title: The Wolfman
rt_critics_consensus: Suitably grand and special effects-laden, The Wolfman suffers from a suspense-deficient script and a surprising lack of genuine chills.

title: The Cathedral
rt_critics_consensus: As carefully and impressively constructed as its title would suggest, The Cathedral offers an absorbing, impressionistic child's-eye look at family life.

title: Blood
rt_critics_consensus: Blood has a handful of solid scares, but a narrative that fails to truly coagulate makes this half-empty vial of vampire horror difficult to recommend.

title: The Ritual
rt_critics_consensus: The Ritual purportedly presents a real occult occurrence, but the most blasphemous thing about it may be wasting Al Pacino's talents on a clichéd story with uninspired execution.

title: Bleed with Me
rt_critics_consensus: An auspicious debut for writer-director Amelia Moses, the smartly restrained Bleed with Me taps a rich vein of psychological horror.

title: Sisters
rt_critics_consensus: Clever yet clearly indebted to the masters of the genre, Sisters offers an early glimpse of De Palma at his stylishly crafty peak.

title: Old Man
rt_critics_consensus: Stephen Lang is as magnetic as ever, but his finely layered performance isn't quite enough to distract from Old Man's predictable story.

title: Haunter
rt_critics_consensus: Haunter's premise is intriguing enough, though the film's too low budget to truly pull it off.

title: The Queen of Black Magic
rt_critics_consensus: The Queen of Black Magic mixes buried trauma with supernatural horror to produce a dark blend that genre fans will savor.

title: Iris
rt_critics_consensus: A small, insightful feature with a deceptively thoughtful thesis, Iris adds a bittersweet postscript to director Albert Maysles' filmography.

title: House of Wax
rt_critics_consensus: Bearing little resemblance to the 1953 original, House of Wax is a formulaic but better-than-average teen slasher flick.

title: In the Tall Grass
rt_critics_consensus: A potentially intriguing premise is rapidly lost in the weeds during In the Tall Grass which struggles to stretch its slim source material to feature length.

title: Big Bad Wolves
rt_critics_consensus: With just enough black humor to lighten the disturbing subject matter, Big Bad Wolves is as rewarding as it is challenging.

title: The Mermaid: Lake of the Dead
Time Share
rt_critics_consensus: Sharp, funny, and delightfully dark, Time Share (Tiempo Compartido) offers genre fans a complex thriller that sinks its hooks in fast and doesn't let go until the closing credits.

title: Censor
rt_critics_consensus: Occasionally uneven but bold and viscerally effective, Censor marks a bloody good step forward for British horror.

title: Revelation Road 2: The Sea of Glass and Fire
Voyeur
rt_critics_consensus: Absorbing, unpredictable, and overall compelling, Voyeur is a singularly unusual -- and utterly memorable -- documentary experience.

title: The Last Will and Testament of Rosalind Leigh
Tusk
rt_critics_consensus: Tusk is pleasantly ridiculous and charmingly self-deprecating, but that isn't enough to compensate for its thin, overstretched story.

title: Gaia
rt_critics_consensus: If its narrative ambitions aren't always equaled by its chilling visuals, Gaia remains an effective slice of ecological horror.

title: Texas Chainsaw Massacre
rt_critics_consensus: Texas Chainsaw Massacre doesn't skimp on the gore, but Leatherface may have irrevocably lost his ability to terrify.

title: Spell
rt_critics_consensus: Despite a promising beginning, this repetitive and predictable thriller lacks enough of an identity to cast much of a Spell.

title: Wolf
rt_critics_consensus: Despite George MacKay's best efforts, Wolf remains a horror-tinged social allegory that's left to largely chase its own tail.

title: The Nun
rt_critics_consensus: The Nun boasts strong performances, spooky atmospherics, and a couple decent set-pieces, but its sins include inconsistent logic and narrative slackness.

title: The Surrogate
rt_critics_consensus: Led by an outstanding performance from Jasmine Batchelor, The Surrogate probes the heart of a thorny dilemma with strength and sensitivity.

title: Mama
rt_critics_consensus: If you're into old-school scares over cheap gore, you'll be able to get over Mama's confusing script and contrived plot devices.

title: The Cellar
rt_critics_consensus: Although it doesn't tumble to The Cellar of the haunted house genre, this is a mostly musty exercise in second-rate supernatural horror.

title: Origin
rt_critics_consensus: A moving drama that's unafraid to ask big questions, Origin honors its source material with powerful performances in service of a deeply emotional story.

title: Infinity Pool
rt_critics_consensus: Turbulent waters even for strong swimmers, Infinity Pool provides a visceral all-inclusive retreat of Cronenbergian perversion for those wanting to escape commercial sundries.

title: Cuckoo
rt_critics_consensus: Directed with inventive flair by Tilman Singer while given flavorful personality by stars Hunter Schafer and Dan Stevens, Cuckoo is a madcap madhouse horror that's on the right side of deranged.

title: Peeping Tom
rt_critics_consensus: Peeping Tom is a chilling, methodical look at the psychology of a killer, and a classic work of voyeuristic cinema.

title: Low Tide
rt_critics_consensus: While familiar and uneven at times, Low Tide is an atmospheric coming-of-age yarn lifted by strong performances from its young cast and a firm directorial hand from newcomer Kevin McMullin.

title: Legion
rt_critics_consensus: Despite a solid cast and intermittent thrills, Legion suffers from a curiously languid pace, confused plot, and an excess of dialogue.

title: Opus
rt_critics_consensus: Malkovich is clearly having a ball playing a nefarious pop musician in Opus, but unfortunately the rest of this thriller is too conceptually confused for the star's fun to prove infectious.

title: Overlord
rt_critics_consensus: Part revisionist war drama, part zombie thriller, and part all-out genre gorefest, Overlord offers A-level fun for B-movie fans of multiple persuasions.

title: Inside
rt_critics_consensus: With unflinching authenticity and haunting, deeply layered performances, Inside transforms its stark prison-based narrative into a gripping, emotionally resonant study of violence, regret, and the fragile possibility of redemption.

title: Life
rt_critics_consensus: Life is just thrilling, well-acted, and capably filmed enough to overcome an overall inability to add new wrinkles to the trapped-in-space genre.

title: Swallowed
rt_critics_consensus: Although its second half is difficult to digest, Swallowed is an admirably unusual -- and overall effective -- mixture of body horror and romance.

title: Your Vice Is a Locked Room and Only I Have the Key
Close Your Eyes
rt_critics_consensus: A near-masterpiece for director/co-writer Victor Erice, Close Your Eyes ends his long gap between films with a moving meditation on memory, identity, and cinema itself.

title: The Call
rt_critics_consensus: The Call builds plenty of suspense before taking a problematic turn in the third act.

title: The Collection
rt_critics_consensus: The Collection offers more grisly thrills and twisted humor than its predecessor; in other words, fun for genre fans, but unpleasant for anyone else.

title: Escape From Tomorrow
rt_critics_consensus: Conceptually audacious but only intermittently successful in execution, Escape From Tomorrow is nonetheless visually inventive and darkly surreal.

title: One Missed Call
rt_critics_consensus: One Missed Call has a few interesting ideas and benefits from director Takashi Miike's eye, but is ultimately too unoriginal to recommend.

title: Our House
rt_critics_consensus: Our House sits on a solid foundation of genuine pathos, even if the storytelling structure tends to be frustratingly rickety in spots.

title: The Killer
rt_critics_consensus: The Killer finds director David Fincher on firm footing with a stylish and engaging thriller that proves a perfect match for leading man Michael Fassbender.

title: Cabin Fever
rt_critics_consensus: No need for a quarantine -- enthusiasm for this inert remake is not contagious.

title: The Pyramid
rt_critics_consensus: Poorly lit and thinly writ, The Pyramid houses little more than clunky dialogue, amateurish acting, and dusty found-footage scares.

title: The Burial
rt_critics_consensus: A solid courtroom drama led by a pair of nicely contrasted performances, The Burial hits the expected genre beats -- and remains a crowd-pleasing treat at every turn.

title: Starfish
rt_critics_consensus: Led by a strong central performance from Virginia Gardner, Starfish is an intelligent, emotionally affecting, and uniquely expansive sci-fi/horror hybrid.

title: Don't Breathe
rt_critics_consensus: Don't Breathe smartly twists its sturdy premise to offer a satisfyingly tense, chilling addition to the home invasion genre that's all the more effective for its simplicity.

title: Glorious
rt_critics_consensus: With thought-provoking themes lurking beneath its absurd premise, Glorious is a brightly blood-spattered genre treat from director Rebekah McKendry.

title: Speak No Evil
rt_critics_consensus: A social satire with razor-sharp teeth, Speak No Evil offers a darkly delicious treat for fans of misanthropic thrillers.

title: I Saw the Devil
rt_critics_consensus: Never flinching during its descent into depravity, I Saw the Devil is a pulverizing thriller that will give bloody satisfaction to audiences who like their revenge served with fiery rage.

title: Scream
rt_critics_consensus: The fifth Scream finds the franchise working harder than ever to maintain its meta edge -- and succeeding surprisingly often.

title: Blood Relatives
rt_critics_consensus: A father-daughter road trip with a vampiric twist, Blood Relatives is one horror-comedy genre mashup that sucks in all the right ways.

title: The Home
rt_critics_consensus: The Home drowns its intriguing premise in a mire of muddled storytelling, illogical twists, and lifeless performances, resulting in a frustrating and tensionless mess.

title: Phantom of the Opera
rt_critics_consensus: Though it lives beneath the 1925 version, Claude Rains plays title character well in this landmark color version of the classic tragedy.

title: Kindred
rt_critics_consensus: Kindred's naturalistic approach may frustrate viewers seeking jump scares, but this psychologically driven horror story casts its own grim spell.

title: Playdate
rt_critics_consensus: Playdate is a bewildering mix of dated machismo, dull comedy, and chaotic action.

title: The Eyes of My Mother
rt_critics_consensus: The Eyes of My Mother uses a shocking trauma to fuel a hauntingly hypnotic odyssey whose nightmarish chill lingers long after the closing credits.

title: Shake Rattle and Roll Fourteen: The Invasion
Seeds
rt_critics_consensus: Quietly powerful and deeply humane, Seeds' patient rhythms and luminous imagery honor generational farmers with an elegiac grace that feels both historically vital and vibrantly alive.

title: Alpha
rt_critics_consensus: Strong performances and a visually striking finale notwithstanding, Alpha ultimately frustrates with its unwieldy structure and imbalanced blend of fantastical and realistic elements.

title: Nosferatu
rt_critics_consensus: Marvelously orchestrated by director Robert Eggers, Nosferatu is a behemoth of a horror film that is equal parts repulsive and seductive.

title: New Life
rt_critics_consensus: Breathing New Life to the genre, this excellently acted brooding horror thriller marks a propitious debut for John Rosman.

title: The Promise
rt_critics_consensus: The Promise wastes an outstanding cast and powerful real-life story on a love triangle that frustratingly fails to engage.

title: Black Death
rt_critics_consensus: Black Death delivers the fire and brimstone violence its subject matter warrants, while posing some interesting questions of faith and religion.

title: Mom and Dad
rt_critics_consensus: Mom and Dad's gonzo premise serves as an effective springboard for a wickedly dark, bloody comedy - and an appropriately over-the-top performance from Nicolas Cage.

title: The Purge: Anarchy
rt_critics_consensus: Gritty, grisly, and uncommonly ambitious, The Purge: Anarchy represents a slight improvement over its predecessor, but it's still never as smart or resonant as it tries to be.

title: The Cell
rt_critics_consensus: The Cell offers disturbing, stunning eye candy, but its visual pleasures are no match for a confused storyline that undermines the movie's inventive aesthetic.

title: The Beach House
rt_critics_consensus: An intriguing and unsettling debut for writer-director Jeffrey A. Brown, The Beach House offers a delightfully grim getaway for fans of imaginative, ambitious horror.

title: #Alive
rt_critics_consensus: Well-acted, genuinely unsettling, and occasionally even funny, #alive proves the crowded zombie genre still has fresh stories to tell.

title: The Ritual
rt_critics_consensus: Director David Bruckner makes evocative use of the Scandinavian setting and a dedicated cast to deliver a handsome -- if familiar -- horror story.

title: Fresh
rt_critics_consensus: As gripping as it is upsetting, Fresh makes a provocative meal out of the horror of modern dating.

title: Jigsaw
rt_critics_consensus: Jigsaw definitely won't win many converts to the Saw franchise, but for longtime fans, it should prove a respectably revolting -- if rarely scary -- diversion.

title: The Exorcism
rt_critics_consensus: The power of Crowe compels up to a point in this metatextual riff on the possession genre, but The Exorcism's greatest sin is ultimately falling back on clichés.

title: Eli
rt_critics_consensus: Intermittently effective if not wholly successful, Eli offers horror fans a handful of jump scares in search of a truly terrifying story.

title: The Devil Went Down to Islington
Run
rt_critics_consensus: Solid acting and expertly ratcheted tension help Run transcend its familiar trappings to deliver a delightfully suspenseful thriller.

title: Jacob's Ladder
rt_critics_consensus: A needless remake that quickly loses sight of the themes that elevated the original, this is a Jacob's Ladder that leads straight to nowhere.

title: Son
rt_critics_consensus: A horror story with unexpected emotional resonance, Son uses its familial focus to counter a certain queasy familiarity.

title: Haunt
rt_critics_consensus: Haunt is spooked by the spirits of its obvious influences, but still packs enough thrills and chills to satisfy horror fans up for a haunted house excursion.

title: The Wheel
rt_critics_consensus: Beautifully filmed and brought to life by a quartet of fine performances, The Wheel spins a quietly moving story about love and relationships.

title: Studio 666
rt_critics_consensus: Studio 666 doesn't quite take its horror-comedy hybrid to 11, but if you're in the mood, this cheerfully over-the-top outing is a lot of fun.

title: Nostalgia
rt_critics_consensus: Nostalgia combines talented actors and honorable intentions, but poky pacing and a lack of depth make it unlikely the end result will ever inspire the titular emotion.

title: Ready or Not
rt_critics_consensus: Smart, subversive, and darkly funny, Ready or Not is a crowd-pleasing horror film with giddily entertaining bite.

title: Too Late
rt_critics_consensus: A horror comedy that's heavier on the laughs, Too Late proves monster movies and show business satire can go together frightfully well.

title: Barbarian
rt_critics_consensus: Smart, darkly humorous, and above all scary, Barbarian offers a chilling and consistently unpredictable thrill ride for horror fans.

title: X
rt_critics_consensus: A fresh spin on the classic slasher formula, X marks the spot where Ti West gets resoundingly back to his horror roots.

title: Men
rt_critics_consensus: If its narrative and thematic reach sometimes exceeds its grasp, magnetic performances from a stellar cast help Men make the most of its horror provocations.

title: Demonic
rt_critics_consensus: Neill Blomkamp's return to low-budget, high-concept filmmaking bungles some interesting ideas, adding up to another Demonic waste of the writer-director's once-promising talent.

title: The Long Walk
rt_critics_consensus: Cooper Hoffman and David Johnsson's soulful performances bring a lot of heart to Stephen King's dystopian tale, making The Long Walk a life-or-death ordeal for its characters but a riveting ride for audiences.

title: Immortal Kiss: Queen of the Night
Flatliners
rt_critics_consensus: Flatliners falls flat as a horror movie and fails to improve upon its source material, rendering this reboot dead on arrival.

title: Knock Knock
rt_critics_consensus: Knock Knock brings a lot of talent to bear on its satirical approach to torture horror, but not effectively enough to overcome its repetitive story or misguidedly campy tone.

title: Reality
rt_critics_consensus: Reality's narrow scope and gripping fact-based story add up to a riveting showcase for Sydney Sweeney in the title role.

title: Never Let Go
rt_critics_consensus: Director Alexandre Aja's latest horror offering boasts anxiety-inducing atmosphere and a committed performance from Halle Berry, although audiences will have to let go of some storytelling expectations to enjoy the ride.

title: Extraterrestrial
rt_critics_consensus: Extraterrestial does deliver a certain amount of gory genre thrills, but they're outweighed by the crushing clichés of its sagging script.

title: Thanksgiving
rt_critics_consensus: Combining belly-busting humor with delightfully over-the-top gore, Thanksgiving is a feast for grindhouse fans.

title: Rabid
rt_critics_consensus: Rabid reimagines David Cronenberg's original narrative with clear reverence and a fresh aesthetic, but despite its biting first half, ultimately devolves into a fairly conventional genre exercise.

title: The Woman
rt_critics_consensus: Strange, audacious, and aggressive, The Woman is an uneven horror flick that game viewers with a wildly bloody finale.

title: Talk to Me
rt_critics_consensus: With a gripping story and impressive practical effects, Talk to Me spins a terrifically creepy 21st-century horror yarn built on classic foundations.

title: I Will Follow You Into the Dark
Lights Out
rt_critics_consensus: Lights Out makes skillful use of sturdy genre tropes -- and some terrific performances -- for an unsettling, fright-filled experience that delivers superior chills without skimping on story.

title: The Harbinger
rt_critics_consensus: Deftly tapping into pandemic-fueled fears, The Harbinger works within a familiar framework to deliver lingering chills.

title: Crimes of the Future
rt_critics_consensus: Quintessential if not classic Cronenberg, Crimes of the Future finds the director revisiting familiar themes with typically unsettling flair.

title: The Harvest
rt_critics_consensus: The Harvest sows enough well-acted, slow-burning tension to satisfy, even if viewers expecting to reap all-out horror may come away wanting more.

title: The Man Who Laughs
rt_critics_consensus: A meeting of brilliant creative minds, The Man Who Laughs serves as a stellar showcase for the talents of director Paul Leni and star Conrad Veidt.

title: The Turning
rt_critics_consensus: Stylish but muddled, this misbegotten adaptation of Henry James' classic novel will send horror-hungry viewers Turning to the source material instead.

title: Silent Night, Deadly Night
rt_critics_consensus: Silent Night, Deadly Night embraces the franchise's inherent absurdity while surpassing the original with sharper filmmaking, richer characterization, and politically astute, pitch-black satire.

title: Green Room
rt_critics_consensus: Green Room delivers unapologetic genre thrills with uncommon intelligence and powerfully acted élan.

title: Candle in the Tomb: The Worm Valley
One for the Road
rt_critics_consensus: It's overlong and uneven, but One for the Road may still satisfy viewers in the mood for a road trip movie with mortality on its mind.

title: Wrong Turn
rt_critics_consensus: Wrong Turn is a cut below more effective horror outings, but viewers in the mood for some gory chills will find that this franchise reboot does more than a few things right.

title: AfrAId
rt_critics_consensus: Repeating the beats of better techno-horrors, AfrAId short circuits due to the clichéd software of its script and the uninspired hardware of its craft.

title: Little Monsters
rt_critics_consensus: Led by typically outstanding work from Lupita Nyong'o, Little Monsters is a horror/rom-com hybrid that proves the zombie genre still has fresh brains to savor.

title: The Truth
rt_critics_consensus: The Truth may not stand with Hirokazu Kore-eda's best work, but it finds the writer-director revisiting familiar themes with a typically sensitive touch.

title: Evolution
rt_critics_consensus: Creepy, provocative, and aesthetically absorbing, Evolution marks a satisfying step forward for director/co-writer Lucile Hadzihalilovic.

title: The Hand That Rocks The Cradle
rt_critics_consensus: A flat and unnecessary remake that squanders its premise on convoluted plotting and meager thrills, The Hand That Rocks The Cradle is salvaged only slightly by Maika Monroe's committed performance.

title: Separation
rt_critics_consensus: A lifeless divorce drama cosplaying as a horror film, Separation is a disjointed mess that fails to escape its formulaic trappings.

title: Truth
rt_critics_consensus: Truth's terrific cast and compelling message are often enough to overcome its occasionally didactic and facile dramatization of a nuanced real-life tale.

title: The Monster
rt_critics_consensus: The Monster uses its effectively simple setup and a powerful lead performance from Zoe Kazan to deliver a traditional yet subtly subversive -- and thoroughly entertaining -- horror story.

title: Resurrection
rt_critics_consensus: Uneven yet steadily absorbing, Resurrection benefits greatly from Rebecca Hall's outstanding work in the leading role.

title: Candyman
rt_critics_consensus: Candyman takes an incisive, visually thrilling approach to deepening the franchise's mythology -- and terrifying audiences along the way.

title: Monolith
rt_critics_consensus: Carried by Lily Sullivan's outstanding lead performance and enriched by an expertly administered sense of creeping claustrophobia, Monolith is an eerie thriller that burns slow and lingers.

title: The Circle
rt_critics_consensus: The Circle assembles an impressive cast, but this digitally driven thriller spins aimlessly in its half-hearted exploration of timely themes.

title: Sleep
rt_critics_consensus: Elegantly executed, Sleep builds prosperously upon a familiar premise and delivers rousing chills.

title: Morgan
rt_critics_consensus: Morgan neglects to develop its decent premise, opting instead to settle for a garden-variety sci-fi thriller with more action than ideas.

title: The Stranger
rt_critics_consensus: Cleverly contextualizing Albert Camus' existentialist classic within a modern political lens, François Ozon's The Stranger is as intriguing to mull over as it is beautiful to look at.

title: The Little Stranger
rt_critics_consensus: The Little Stranger's reliance on atmosphere may satisfy audiences in the mood for sophisticated horror fare -- while frustrating those seeking more visceral thrills.

title: Tótem
rt_critics_consensus: A heartfelt film that tells a poignant story without straying into sentimentality, Tótem is a life-affirming triumph for writer-director Lila Avilés.

title: Sputnik
rt_critics_consensus: Effective space alien horror with a Soviet-era twist, Sputnik proves there are still some scary good sci-fi thrillers left in the galaxy.

title: It's Alive
rt_critics_consensus: Tough and unpleasant, It's Alive throttles the viewer with its bizarre mutant baby theatrics.

title: Shortcut
rt_critics_consensus: This road trip horror outing may take one Shortcut too many to count as a truly satisfying journey, but refreshing brevity and a show-don't-tell approach smooth the ride.

title: Juan of the Dead
rt_critics_consensus: Filled with wild splatter slapstick, Juan of the Dead also deftly uses its zombie premise as an undead Trojan horse for insightful political commentary.

title: Finders Keepers
rt_critics_consensus: As bizarrely hilarious as it is insightful, Finders Keepers is a stranger-than-fiction gem of a documentary that transcends its oddball trappings.

title: Sinners
rt_critics_consensus: A rip-roaring fusion of masterful visual storytelling and toe-tapping music, writer-director Ryan Coogler's first original blockbuster reveals the full scope of his singular imagination.

title: Slice
rt_critics_consensus: Wildly imaginative and powered by Chance The Rapper's bright charisma, Slice piles on ideas with infectious energy, but can't always overcome its overstuffed plot and uneven pacing.

title: Wounds
rt_critics_consensus: Wounds isn't without its creepy-crawly charms, but they -- and the efforts of a talented cast -- get squished by a story that never quite gets completely under the skin.

title: Until Dawn
rt_critics_consensus: Until Dawn has a novel premise by rewinding the clock repeatedly to deliver multiple horror movies in one, but a lack of inspiration in each new variation yields a spoil of diminishing returns rather than riches.

title: Beast
rt_critics_consensus: Want to watch Idris Elba fight a lion? The admirably lean yet ultimately disposable Beast is just the movie you're looking for.

title: Blumhouse's Fantasy Island
rt_critics_consensus: Fantasy Island tries to show audiences the dark side of wish fulfillment, but mainly serves as a cautionary tale about the dangers of exhuming long-dead franchises.

title: The Forest
rt_critics_consensus: The Forest offers Natalie Dormer a few chances to showcase her range in a dual role, but they aren't enough to offset the fact that the movie's simply not all that scary.

title: Dark Skies
rt_critics_consensus: Dark Skies writer director Scott Stewart has a solid cast, an interesting premise, and some admirable ambitions, but he can't figure out what to do with any of them, and the result is a dull, muddled effort that will bore all but the most devoted horror buffs.

title: What Have They Done to Your Daughters?
Don't Look Back
rt_critics_consensus: Don't Look Back does an impressive job of working within obvious budget constraints, but writer-director Jeffrey Reddick is overly reliant on genre clichés.

title: He Sees You When You're Sleeping
Trick
rt_critics_consensus: Fast-paced savagery and a memorable twist aren't enough to make up for Trick's slavish devotion to superior slasher films of the past.

title: Beware the Moon: Remembering 'An American Werewolf in London'
Goodnight Mommy
rt_critics_consensus: Naomi Watts is as talented as ever, but this Goodnight Mommy remake has no real reason to exist.

title: Triggered
rt_critics_consensus: Triggered's combustible premise largely fizzles out thanks to thinly written characters and a story that's heavier on fast-paced violence than actual ideas.

title: Paranoia
rt_critics_consensus: Clichéd and unoriginal, Paranoia is a middling techno-thriller with indifferent performances and a shortage of thrills.

title: Excision
rt_critics_consensus: Excision effectively blends body horror and adolescent drama, although its visceral aggression definitely isn't for all tastes.

title: The Haunting
rt_critics_consensus: Sophisticated visual effects fail to offset awkward performances and an uneven script in The Haunting

title: Y2K
rt_critics_consensus: Y2K earns points for ambition and sheer audacity, even if it struggles to keep the laughs coming while maintaining a messy tonal blend.

title: Trap
rt_critics_consensus: An arch thriller given some grounding by Josh Hartnett's committed performance, Shyamalan's Trap will ensnare those who appreciate its tongue-in-cheek style while the rest will be eager to wriggle out from it.

title: We Are What We Are
rt_critics_consensus: A compelling story cleverly told, We Are What We Are quenches horror buffs' thirst for gore while serving up serious-minded filmmaking and solid acting.

title: Escape Room
rt_critics_consensus: Escape Room fails to unlock much of the potential in its premise, but what's left is still tense and thrilling enough to offer a passing diversion for suspense fans.

title: A Quiet Place
rt_critics_consensus: A Quiet Place artfully plays on elemental fears with a ruthlessly intelligent creature feature that's as original as it is scary -- and establishes director John Krasinski as a rising talent.

title: Piggy
rt_critics_consensus: Brought hauntingly to life by Laura Galán's committed performance, Piggy deftly deploys genre thrills in service of sharp social commentary.

title: Borderline
rt_critics_consensus: Elevated by Samara Weaving and Ray Nicholson's performances to a point, this fun-enough romp eventually finds itself on the Borderline.

title: Rings
rt_critics_consensus: Rings may offer ardent fans of the franchise a few threadbare thrills, but for everyone else, it may feel like an endless loop of muddled mythology and rehashed plot points.

title: DogMan
rt_critics_consensus: While Besson, Landry Jones, and a pack of mutts lend DogMan their roaring souls, this entry offers ample creative risks and kibbled returns.

title: Apostle
rt_critics_consensus: Apostle resists easy scares in favor of a steady, slow-building descent into dread led by a commanding central performance from Dan Stevens.

title: Nefarious
rt_critics_consensus: Nefarious is well-mounted and boasts some solid performances, but it's hard to look past the story's heavy-handed proselytizing.

title: Mother's Day
rt_critics_consensus: Arguably well-intended yet thoroughly misguided, Mother's Day is the cinematic equivalent of a last-minute gift that only underscores its embarrassing lack of effort.

title: Queen of Spades: The Dark Rite
Hellraiser
rt_critics_consensus: A gift for long-suffering fans after numerous subpar sequels, David Bruckner's Hellraiser unlocks the puzzle box for getting this franchise back on the right track.

title: Presence
rt_critics_consensus: A slow-burning spectral thriller, Presence reaffirms that Soderbergh plays with form as deftly as he flits between genres.

title: The Keeper
rt_critics_consensus: The Keeper strays into easy sentimentality, but this fact-based drama's warm spirit makes its indulgences easy to forgive.

title: Hell Baby
rt_critics_consensus: Though its brand of immature comedy may appeal to some, Hell Baby misses the mark with much of its humor, rendering it a largely ineffective -- and often crass -- genre sendup.

title: The Witch
rt_critics_consensus: As thought-provoking as it is visually compelling, The Witch delivers a deeply unsettling exercise in slow-building horror that suggests great things for debuting writer-director Robert Eggers.

title: Split
rt_critics_consensus: Split serves as a dramatic tour de force for James McAvoy in multiple roles -- and finds writer-director M. Night Shyamalan returning resoundingly to thrilling form.

title: The Last Exorcism Part II
rt_critics_consensus: The Last Exorcism Part II ditches the found footage format for relatively earnest but deadly tedious psychological terror that devolves into hokum anyways.

title: Raw
rt_critics_consensus: Raw's lurid violence and sexuality live up to its title, but they're anchored with an immersive atmosphere and deep symbolism that linger long after the provocative visuals fade.

title: Exit 8
rt_critics_consensus: An unsettling maze navigated with finesse by director Genki Kawamura, Exit 8 is a video game adaptation rendered with existential dread and stylistic sophistication.

title: Ouija
rt_critics_consensus: Slowly, steadily, although no one seems to be moving it in that direction, the Ouija planchette points to NO.

title: Insidious: Chapter 3
rt_critics_consensus: Insidious: Chapter 3 isn't as terrifying as the original, although it boasts surprising thematic depth and is enlivened by another fine performance from Lin Shaye.

title: The Crow
rt_critics_consensus: Dreary and poorly paced, this reimagining of The Crow doesn't have enough personality or pulse to merit the resurrection.

title: Seance
rt_critics_consensus: Writer-director Simon Barrett has a certain filmmaking flair, but it isn't enough to raise Seance above its somewhat pedestrian story.

title: Bliss
rt_critics_consensus: Bliss may be too narratively and visually intense for less adventurous viewers, but should trigger the titular state in those with a taste for hard-hitting horror.

title: Malignant
rt_critics_consensus: Although Malignant isn't particularly scary, director James Wan's return to horror contains plenty of gory thrills -- and a memorably bonkers twist.

title: Lamb
rt_critics_consensus: Darkly imaginative and brought to life by a pair of striking central performances, Lamb shears expectations with its singularly wooly chills.

title: The Unholy
rt_critics_consensus: Rarely scary and often dull, The Unholy falls back on the same tired tropes that have already been done to death by countless other religious horror movies.

title: The Night
rt_critics_consensus: The dramatic heft and dread of The Night delivers haunting psychological frights -- and an impressive feature directorial debut for Kourosh Ahari.

title: Maggie
rt_critics_consensus: Maggie lurches a bit clumsily at times, but is partially redeemed by strong performances and an unexpectedly thoughtful tone.

title: It Lives Inside
rt_critics_consensus: Effective horror with a strong core of social commentary, It Lives Inside is an eerily compelling calling card for filmmaker Bishal Dutta in his feature-length debut.

title: I Can Make You Love Me
The End? (In un giorno la fine)

rt_critics_consensus: Thanks to director Zak Hilditch's patient storytelling and strong work from lead Thomas Jane, 1922 ranks among the more satisfying Stephen King adaptations.

title: Wolf Man
rt_critics_consensus: Director Leigh Whannell's attempt at bringing a fresh psychological dimension to the Wolf Man comes at the expense of proper scares, although fans of body horror will still find some tasty morsels to chew on.

title: The Curse of La Llorona
rt_critics_consensus: Content to coast on jump scares rather than tap into its story's creepy potential, The Curse of La Llorona arrives in theaters already broken.

title: Underwater
rt_critics_consensus: Underwater's strong cast and stylish direction aren't enough to distract from the strong sense of déjà vu provoked by this claustrophobic thriller's derivative story.

title: Satanic Panic
rt_critics_consensus: It isn't terribly devilish or particularly pulse-quickening, but Satanic Panic remains an entertaining midnight movie that forgiving genre enthusiasts will enjoy.

title: The Bay
rt_critics_consensus: Barry Levinson's eco-horror flick cleverly utilizes familiar found-footage methods in service of a gruesome yet atmospheric chiller.

title: EMMA.
rt_critics_consensus: Other adaptations may do a better job of consistently capturing the spirit of the classic source material, but Jane Austen fans should still find a solid match in this Emma.

title: The Wicker Man
rt_critics_consensus: This intelligent horror film is subtle in its thrills and chills, with an ending that is both shocking and truly memorable.

title: The Lonely Man With The Ghost Machine
Firestarter
rt_critics_consensus: There was plenty of room to improve on the original, but Firestarter trips over that low bar and tumbles toward the bottom of the long list of Stephen King adaptations.

title: Monstrous
rt_critics_consensus: Christina Ricci is capable of carrying a lot, but even her talents are tested by this often Monstrous-ly dull horror movie.

title: Ash
rt_critics_consensus: Flying Lotus' Ash delivers the phantasmagorical goods with vivid visuals and a throbbing soundscape, elevating a predictable sci-fi story into a memorably stylish head-trip.

title: Pet Sematary
rt_critics_consensus: Pet Sematary takes its source material in a few different directions, but this remake feels like an exhuming almost as often as it does a revival.

title: The Game
rt_critics_consensus: The ending could use a little work but this is otherwise another sterling example of David Fincher's iron grip on atmosphere and storytelling.

title: Goodnight Mommy
rt_critics_consensus: Dark, violent, and drenched in dread, Goodnight Mommy is perfect for extreme horror enthusiasts -- or filmgoers who prefer to watch between splayed fingers.

title: Swallow
rt_critics_consensus: Swallow's unconventional approach to exploring domestic ennui is elevated by a well-told story and Haley Bennett's powerful leading performance.

title: Where the Devil Roams
rt_critics_consensus: Grim and gratifyingly ambitious, Where the Devil Roams finds the Adams Family tapping a Gothic vein filled with lingering horror.

title: Blood Riders: The Devil Rides with Us
The Invasion
rt_critics_consensus: The Invasion is slickly made, but it lacks psychological insight and thrills.

title: Locked
rt_critics_consensus: Matching up Bill Skarsgård and Anthony Hopkins in a battle of wits, Locked is loaded with plenty of clever jolts even if it doesn't quite know where to park its story.

title: A Cat in the Brain (Un gatto nel cervello) (Nightmare Concert)
Mary Shelley's Frankenstein
rt_critics_consensus: Mary Shelley's Frankenstein is ambitious and visually striking, but the overwrought tone and lack of scares make for a tonally inconsistent experience.

title: The Fearless Vampire Killers or: Pardon Me, but Your Teeth Are in My Neck
rt_critics_consensus: This uneven but amiable 1967 vampire picture is part horror spoof, part central European epic, and 100 percent Roman Polanski, whose signature sensibility colors every frame.

title: Twisted
rt_critics_consensus: An implausible, overheated potboiler that squanders a stellar cast, Twisted is a clichéd, risible whodunit.

title: Humane
rt_critics_consensus: Humane's twisted psychological thrills are diverting enough in the moment to distract from its occasionally awkward attempts to balance sociopolitical commentary against high-concept horror.

title: Faceless (Los Depredadores de la Noche) (Les Prédateurs de la Nuit)
Gremlins
rt_critics_consensus: Whether you choose to see it as a statement on consumer culture or simply a special effects-heavy popcorn flick, Gremlins is a minor classic.

title: Troy
rt_critics_consensus: A brawny, entertaining spectacle, but lacking emotional resonance.

title: The Island
rt_critics_consensus: A clone of THX 1183, Coma, and Logan's Run, The Island is another loud and bombastic Michael Bay movie where explosions and chases matter more than characters, dialogue, or plot.

title: Faces
rt_critics_consensus: Director John Cassavetes forgoes conventional style in this hard-bitten examination of a marriage, letting his actors tell the story through dialogue and their Faces to refreshing effect.

title: Dog of God
rt_critics_consensus: Dog of God blends intense violence, dark humor, and provocative sexuality to deliver a uniquely expressive exploration of sin, religion, and human behavior that's as provocative as it is unforgettable.

title: The Sentinel
rt_critics_consensus: The Sentinel starts off well enough but quickly wears thin with too many plot holes and conventional action sequences.

title: How Far Does the Dark Go?
Once Upon a Time at Christmas
Goosebumps 2: Haunted Halloween
rt_critics_consensus: Goosebumps 2: Haunted Halloween offers a handful of treats for very young viewers, but compared to the entertaining original, this sequel is a ding dong to ditch.

title: Trespass
rt_critics_consensus: Trespass pits public servants against gangsters in a race for stolen loot -- and thanks to a killer cast and Walter Hill's assured direction, the audience wins.

title: The Divine Protector: Master Salt Begins
Her
rt_critics_consensus: Sweet, soulful, and smart, Spike Jonze's Her uses its just-barely-sci-fi scenario to impart wryly funny wisdom about the state of modern human relationships.

title: Mute
rt_critics_consensus: Visually polished but narratively derivative and overall muddled, Mute is a would-be sci-fi epic whose title serves as an unfortunate guide to how it might be best enjoyed.

title: 30 Days of Night
rt_critics_consensus: While 30 Days of Night offers a few thrills, it ultimately succumbs to erratic execution.

title: The Exorcist
rt_critics_consensus: The Exorcist rides its supernatural theme to magical effect, with remarkable special effects and an eerie atmosphere, resulting in one of the scariest films of all time.

title: Unfriend
rt_critics_consensus: Friend Request's attempts to update old-school teen horror for the digital age do not, sadly, include memorable characters, fresh scares, or novel storytelling twists.

title: The Seeding
rt_critics_consensus: Rich atmosphere and a handful of genuinely disturbing moments are only sporadically enough to outweigh The Seeding's monotonous pace and lack of narrative depth.

title: Sleep Tight
rt_critics_consensus: The strong lead performance, clever plot turns, and the unsettling ending makes Sleep Tight worth stalking.

title: Brightburn
rt_critics_consensus: Although Brightburn doesn't fully deliver on the pitch-black promise of its setup, it's still enough to offer a diverting subversion of the superhero genre.

title: The Manson Brothers Midnight Zombie Massacre
La Saga de los Drácula (The Dracula Saga) (Dracula: The Bloodline Continues) (Saga of the Draculas)
Doghouse
rt_critics_consensus: An amateurish, unfunny and unscary British horror-comedy that could be accused of misogyny.

title: Hugo
rt_critics_consensus: Hugo is an extravagant, elegant fantasy with an innocence lacking in many modern kids' movies, and one that emanates an unabashed love for the magic of cinema.

title: 28 Days Later
rt_critics_consensus: Kinetically directed by Danny Boyle, 28 Days Later is both a terrifying zombie movie and a sharp political allegory.

title: I'll Always Know What You Did Last Summer
Black Water: Abyss
rt_critics_consensus: It's unlikely to leave a lasting impression, but Black Water: Abyss may satisfy forgiving genre fans in search of a reasonably effective creature feature.

title: Darkness Falls
rt_critics_consensus: A derivative movie where the scares are few and things don't make much sense.

title: Sarah Landon and the Paranormal Hour
Jason Goes to Hell: The Final Friday
A Tale of Two Sisters
rt_critics_consensus: Restrained but disturbing, A Tale of Two Sisters is a creepily effective, if at times confusing, horror movie.

title: Innocent Blood
rt_critics_consensus: Awkward tonal shifts and a sluggish pace plague Innocent Blood -- a horror-comedy with anemic scares and laughs.

title: The Ice Storm
rt_critics_consensus: Director Ang Lee revisits the ennui-laden decadence of 1970s suburban America with deft humor and gripping pathos.

title: Aquarius
rt_critics_consensus: Led by a powerful performance from Sônia Braga, Aquarius uses a conflict between a tenant and developers to take an insightful look at the relationship between space and identity.

title: Carrie
rt_critics_consensus: It boasts a talented cast, but Kimberly Peirce's "reimagining" of Brian De Palma's horror classic finds little new in the Stephen King novel -- and feels woefully unnecessary.

title: Solo
rt_critics_consensus: Featuring hammy performances and bland characters, Solo is an all too straightforward actioner that's both predictable and instantly forgettable.

title: Prisoners of the Ghostland
rt_critics_consensus: Prisoners of the Ghostland is far from Sono's most distinctive work, but viewers in the mood for a deliriously gonzo genre mash-up featuring an explosive performance from Nicolas Cage just might have a ball.

title: Flux Gourmet
rt_critics_consensus: Flux Gourmet isn't for all tastes, but viewers attuned to writer-director Peter Strickland's unique wavelength will find this horror-laced satire a savory delight.

title: Unknown
rt_critics_consensus: Though it boasts a talented cast, this thriller fails to make you care about the twisty mystery of the men's identities and situation.

title: The Darkest Hour
rt_critics_consensus: Devoid of believable characters or convincing visual effects, this may be The Darkest Hour for the careers of all involved.

title: Lore
rt_critics_consensus: Newcomer Saskia Rosendahl gives an astonishingly assured performance in the title role, and director Cate Shortland establishes a delicate, disturbingly ominous tone in the powerful World War II drama Lore.

title: Dolly
rt_critics_consensus: Packed with grim, relentless set pieces and gleefully excessive gore, Dolly may not reinvent the slasher wheel, but it spins it with such chaotic energy that it becomes an unapologetically brutal celebration of horror's most unhinged extremes.

title: Teeth
rt_critics_consensus: Smart, original, and horrifically funny, Teeth puts a fresh feminist spin on horror movie tropes.

title: Man Finds Tape
rt_critics_consensus: Man Finds Tape is a smart, slow-burning directorial debut that delivers layered, imaginative horror with strong performances and compelling mysteries.

title: Consecration
rt_critics_consensus: Despite being blessed with Jena Malone in the lead, Consecration serves up Catholic-themed horror that's far more hoary than hallowed.

title: Sweeney Todd: The Demon Barber of Fleet Street
Marebito
