rt_critics_consensus: The scares are lacking in this J-horror flick, and the plot soon turns half-baked.

title: Willard
rt_critics_consensus: In this creepy story of a man and his rodents, Glover seems born to play the oddball title character.

title: Razors: The Return of Jack the Ripper
The 'Burbs
rt_critics_consensus: The 'Burbs has an engaging premise, likable cast, and Joe Dante at the helm -- so the mixed-up genre exercise they produce can't help but feel like a disappointment.

title: Dead Lover
rt_critics_consensus: A bonkers riff on the lengths people will go to for connection, Dead Lover's singular sensibility can be an acquired taste but writer-director Grace Glowicki's vision is admirably one of a kind.

title: Dead Calm
rt_critics_consensus: Nicole Kidman's coiled intensity and muscular direction by Phillip Noyce give this nautical thriller a disquieting sense of dread.

title: Life's a Bitch Then You Die
Stalked by My Doctor: A Sleepwalker's Nightmare
Traitor
rt_critics_consensus: Despite another reliable performance from Don Cheadle, Traitor suffers from too many cliches and an unfocused narrative.

title: Bug
rt_critics_consensus: Disappointing resolution aside, Bug uses its claustrophobic setting and cinéma vérité camerawork to tense, impressive effect.

title: The Hitcher
rt_critics_consensus: Its journey is never quite as revelatory as it could be, but The Hitcher stands as a white-knuckle vision of horror, bolstered by Rutger Hauer's menacing performance.

title: The Wolf House
rt_critics_consensus: Surreal, unsettling, and finally haunting, The Wolf House is a stunning outpouring of creativity whose striking visuals queasily complement its disturbing story.

title: Kade Dade Diyan Kade Pote Diyan
I'll See You in My Dreams
rt_critics_consensus: I'll See You in My Dreams would be worth watching even if Blythe Danner's central performance was all it had going for it, but this thoughtful drama satisfies on multiple levels.

title: Date Night
rt_critics_consensus: An uneasy blend of action and comedy, Date Night doesn't quite live up to the talents of its two leads, but Steve Carell and Tina Fey still manage to shine through most of the movie's flaws.

title: Time Cut
rt_critics_consensus: Not as fun as its initial premise promises, this time traveling slasher doesn't execute well, delivering a forgettable Time Cut.

title: Alien 3
rt_critics_consensus: Alien³ takes admirable risks with franchise mythology, but far too few pay off in a thinly scripted sequel whose stylish visuals aren't enough to enliven a lack of genuine thrills.

title: USAH: Uncommon Stories of American Horror
Click
rt_critics_consensus: This Adam Sandler vehicle borrows shamelessly from It's A Wonderful Life and Back To The Future, and fails to produce the necessary laughs that would forgive such imitation.

title: Ten Minutes to Midnight
rt_critics_consensus: Led by Caroline Williams' outstanding performance, the entertainingly old-school Ten Minutes to Midnight delivers bloody good fun for horror fans.

title: 28 Weeks Later
rt_critics_consensus: While 28 Weeks Later lacks the humanism that made 28 Days Later a classic, it's made up with fantastic atmosphere and punchy direction.

title: The Man in the White Van
The Soul Eater
rt_critics_consensus: An elegantly crafted horror procedural tinged with fairy tale elements, The Soul Eater uses subtle performances and lingering dread to deliver an unflinching, haunting portrait of hidden evil.

title: Honey
rt_critics_consensus: An attractive Jessica Alba and energetic dance numbers provide some lift to this corny and formulaic movie.

title: Ghost Story
rt_critics_consensus: Director John Irvin wastes the efforts of a tremendously talented cast, making this one Ghost Story that deserves to be drowned out by boos.

title: The Killer With A Thousand Eyes
Respiro
rt_critics_consensus: A sweet, but slight picture filled with beautiful people and locations.

title: Are You Here
rt_critics_consensus: Given the amount of talent assembled on both sides of the camera, Are You Here falls bewilderingly flat.

title: The Banishing
rt_critics_consensus: The Banishing never truly taps into its full potential, although this gothic haunted house story still exudes a certain stately chill.

title: Fury of the Demon (La Rage du Démon)
A Nightmare on Elm Street 3: Dream Warriors
rt_critics_consensus: A Nightmare on Elm Street 3: Dream Warriors offers an imaginative and surprisingly satisfying rebound for a franchise already starting to succumb to sequelitis.

title: The Amityville Horror
rt_critics_consensus: Dull and disappointing, the best that can be said for The Amityville Horror is that it set a low bar for its many sequels and remakes.

title: Don't Be Afraid of the Dark
The Cabinet of Dr. Caligari
rt_critics_consensus: Arguably the first true horror film, The Cabinet of Dr. Caligari set a brilliantly high bar for the genre -- and remains terrifying nearly a century after it first stalked the screen.

title: Bring It On: Cheer or Die
Alien: Romulus
rt_critics_consensus: Honoring its nightmarish predecessors while chestbursting at the seams with new frights of its own, Romulus injects some fresh acid blood into one of cinema's great horror franchises.

title: Kill It and Leave This Town
It! The Terror From Beyond Space
Wait Until Dark
rt_critics_consensus: Nail-bitingly tense and brilliantly acted, Wait Until Dark is a compact thriller that makes the most of its fiendishly clever premise.

title: Whistle and I'll Come to You
Santa Sangre
rt_critics_consensus: Those unfamiliar with Alejandro Jodorowsky's style may find it overwhelming, but Santa Sangre is a provocative psychedelic journey featuring the director's signature touches of violence, vulgarity, and an oddly personal moral center.

title: Hell House LLC Origins: The Carmichael Manor
Hooked: The Legend of Demetrius `Hook' Mitchell
Tyler Perry's the Family That Preys
rt_critics_consensus: Tyler Perry's The Family that Preys treads the filmmaker's established terrain of family loyalty and moral uprightness, though the occasionally predictable plot slides forward, aided by an excess of soapy melodrama.

title: Survival of the Dead
rt_critics_consensus: Survival of the Dead offers glimmers of Romero's savage wit, but not nearly enough to make up for his unusually uninspired directing and a lack of new ideas

title: The Investigation: A Haunting in Sherwood
Affliction
rt_critics_consensus: Dark and bleak, the "kick-ass" performances, especially Nolte's "effective" portrayal of an abused soul, is the reason to see this film.

title: The Moogai
rt_critics_consensus: The Moogai attempts to creep its way into the annals of horror history but falls flat under the weight of its poor execution.

title: The Bug: Life and Times of the People's Car
What the Waters Left Behind: Scars
Valentine
rt_critics_consensus: Valentine is basically a formulaic throwback to conventional pre-Scream slasher flicks, which doesn't offer enough suspense or scares to justify its addition to the genre.

title: Fear the Night
rt_critics_consensus: Despite typically strong work from Maggie Q, Fear the Night is a fairly generic and disappointingly tension-free thriller from writer-director Neil LaBute.

title: Under Paris
rt_critics_consensus: Marrying environmental themes with bloody thrills, this Gallic entry into the shark attack canon ultimately lands on the right side of ridiculous fun.

title: The Omen
rt_critics_consensus: Even with the force of a "classic" behind it, remake fever can't hold up the hollowness of this style-drenched Omen.

title: Dollface: The Terror on Route Nine
The Curse of Audrey Earnshaw
rt_critics_consensus: The Curse of Audrey Earnshaw compensates for a certain amount of frustrating narrative drift with solid performances and a strong command of mood.

title: The Monster X Strikes Back: Attack the G8 Summit
Your Monster
rt_critics_consensus: A wickedly delightful genre mashup led by Melissa Barrera, Your Monster is pleasantly packed with songs, guts, and heart.

title: The Last Voyage of the Demeter
rt_critics_consensus: The Last Voyage of the Demeter finds a fresh angle on Dracula's oft-told tale, although lackluster execution often undercuts the story's claustrophobic tension.

title: The Loft
rt_critics_consensus: Populated with characters as unpleasant as its sleazy storyline, The Loft is uninhabitable for all but the least demanding erotic thriller fans.

title: Martyrs
rt_critics_consensus: A real polarising movie, this Gallic torture-porn is graphic, brutal, nasty and gruesome and not to everyone's taste.

title: The Conjuring: The Devil Made Me Do It
rt_critics_consensus: The Devil Made Me Do It represents a comedown for the core Conjuring films, although Vera Farmiga and Patrick Wilson keep the audience invested.

title: Sleepy Hollow
rt_critics_consensus: It isn't Tim Burton's best work, but Sleepy Hollow entertains with its stunning visuals and creepy atmosphere.

title: Night Shift
rt_critics_consensus: Night Shift bristles with pitch-perfect laughs thanks to Ron Howard's snappy direction and a side-splitting turn from Michael Keaton.

title: Bring Her Back
rt_critics_consensus: A domestic nightmare that draws its most profound scares from Sally Hawkins' deranged performance, Bring Her Back is an exemplary chiller that reaffirms directors Danny and Michael Philippou as modern masters of horror.

title: Mil Mascaras vs. the Aztec Mummy
Transylmania
rt_critics_consensus: Never aiming higher than threadbare jokes and offensive attempts at politically incorrect humor, Transylmania is a vampire comedy that truly sucks.

title: The Strange Case of Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde
Erzsabet and the Mystery of Iniquity
Barbarians
rt_critics_consensus: Sporadically suspenseful, Barbarians is too tonally indecisive to consistently make its most important points.

title: Psycho Killer
rt_critics_consensus: Qu'est-ce que c'est? Better run, run, run, run, run, run, run away.

title: St. Agatha
rt_critics_consensus: St. Agatha makes admittedly uneven use of its story's well-worn religious tropes, but the overall effect may still be just chilling enough for horror fans.

title: Slanted
rt_critics_consensus: Slanted blends genre thrills and pointed commentary on racial identity into a daring body horror parable, even if its occasionally unfocused script keeps writer-director Amy Wang's provocative vision from fully landing.

title: The Purge: Election Year
rt_critics_consensus: It isn't particularly subtle, but The Purge: Election Year's blend of potent jolts and timely themes still add up to a nastily effective diversion.

title: Tarnation
rt_critics_consensus: Using music and home video footage to great effect, Jonathan Caouette has made a very personal and moving film.

title: Psycho
rt_critics_consensus: Infamous for its shower scene, but immortal for its contribution to the horror genre. Because Psycho was filmed with tact, grace, and art, Hitchcock didn't just create modern horror, he validated it.

title: The Quiet
rt_critics_consensus: This psychological thriller's talented cast is undercut by leaden pacing and a problematic plot.

title: Pet Sematary: Bloodlines
rt_critics_consensus: Pet Sematary: Bloodlines takes half-hearted stabs at expanding the world of Stephen King's original story, but they're largely lost in a substandard story that mistakes bland gore for effective horror.

title: Joshua
rt_critics_consensus: Though Joshua is ultimately too formulaic, its intelligence and suspenseful buildup heighten the overall creep factor.

title: Bird Box
rt_critics_consensus: Bird Box never quite reaches its intriguing potential, but strong acting and an effectively chilly mood offer intermittently creepy compensation.

title: Come Play
rt_critics_consensus: A frightening feature debut from Jacob Chase, Come Play makes up for its uneven tone by adding real depth to its jarring scares.

title: Castle of Blood (Danza macabra)(Coffin of Terror)(Dimensions in Death)(Tombs of Terror)
The Pool
rt_critics_consensus: Beautifully crafted with loosely drawn characters and a lilting, natural pace, Chris Smith's The Pool features a universal message to which everyone can relate.

title: Red Dragon
rt_critics_consensus: Competently made, but everything is a bit too familiar.

title: Lurker
rt_critics_consensus: Devilishly examining the intersection of fame and obsession to thrilling effect, Alex Russell's directorial debut delivers on all fronts.

title: All Dolled Up: A New York Dolls Story
W Lesie Dzis nie Zasnie Nikt
Paranormal Haunting: The Curse of the Blue Moon Inn
In Search of Darkness: Part II
Love at First Bite
rt_critics_consensus: Love at First Bite could use some more warm-blooded barbs to liven up its undead comedy, but George Hamilton's campy charisma gives the prince of darkness some welcome pizazz.

title: The House and the White String
Donkey Punch
rt_critics_consensus: Donkey Punch begins as a promising thriller, but loses credibility while adhering too closely to genre conventions.

title: The Artist
rt_critics_consensus: A crowd-pleasing tribute to the magic of silent cinema, The Artist is a clever, joyous film with delightful performances and visual style to spare.

title: The Crucifix: Blood of the Exorcist
Devil's Due
rt_critics_consensus: Derivative and mostly uninspired, Devil's Due adds little to either the found-footage or horror genres that it's content to mimic.

title: Water
rt_critics_consensus: This compassionate work of social criticism is also luminous, due to both its lyrical imagery and cast.

title: Dave
rt_critics_consensus: Ivan Reitman's refreshingly earnest political comedy benefits from an understated, charming script and a breezy performance by Kevin Kline.

title: How to Be a Serial Killer
Endless
rt_critics_consensus: While it hopes to make viewers swoon over a bond that defies death, all but the most passionate fans of YA romance may struggle to arouse even puppy love for Endless.

title: El Conde
rt_critics_consensus: A darkly delirious satire rooted in real-life horror, El Conde finds Pablo Larraín revisiting familiar themes without losing their provocative power.

title: The Mummy
rt_critics_consensus: It's difficult to make a persuasive argument for The Mummy as any kind of meaningful cinematic achievement, but it's undeniably fun to watch.

title: Broadcast Signal Intrusion
rt_critics_consensus: Broadcast Signal Intrusion struggles to satisfactorily resolve its setup, but for much of its runtime, it offers an intriguing, well-acted diversion for horror fans.

title: The Green Inferno
rt_critics_consensus: The Green Inferno may not win writer-director Eli Roth many new converts, but fans of his flair for gory spectacle should find it a suitably gruesome diversion.

title: This is GWAR
rt_critics_consensus: This Is GWAR is an entertaining and oddly inspiring documentary that vividly captures the band's grotesque artistry, wild performances, and candid personal stories.

title: How NOT to Make a Horror Film
The Rocky Horror Picture Show: Let's Do the Time Warp Again
rt_critics_consensus: Laverne Cox's fabulous portrayal of Frank N. Furter leads a strong ensemble effort, but the stars can't infuse this reimagining with enough energy, creativity, and quirk to make TRHPS: Let's Do the Time Warp Again a worthwhile endeavor.

title: The Limehouse Golem
rt_critics_consensus: The Limehouse Golem offers old-school Hammer-style horror anchored by rich period detail and strong work from a solid cast.

title: Lunacy
rt_critics_consensus: A Svankmajer movie is not for everyone, but he displays his usual creative flair for surreal imagery.

title: Argento's Dracula
rt_critics_consensus: Schlocky and gross but far from bad enough to be good, Argento's Dracula 3D bites and sucks in all the wrong ways.

title: In the Belly of a Tiger
Children Shouldn't Play With Dead Things
Cracks
rt_critics_consensus: Atomospheric but not much else, Cracks is a formless film in search of compelling drama.

title: American Carnage
rt_critics_consensus: American Carnage is an unsubtle but intermittently sharp horror-comedy whose macabre, politically charged satire is promising yet too thinly spread to fully cohere.

title: The Mortuary Collection
rt_critics_consensus: The rare anthology that maintains a consistently high level of quality, The Mortuary Collection is a must-see undertaking for horror fans.

title: Girl
rt_critics_consensus: Girl uses one aspiring dancer's story as the framework for a poignant drama that approaches its difficult themes with fittingly alluring grace.

title: It Came From Beneath the Sea
The Black Demon
rt_critics_consensus: A small shark in a big pond of man-eating legends, The Black Demon bites off more than it can chew, leaving little carnage for the audience to feast on.

title: The Witches
rt_critics_consensus: With a deliciously wicked performance from Angelica Huston and imaginative puppetry by Jim Henson's creature shop, Nicolas Roeg's dark and witty movie captures the spirit of Roald Dahl's writing like few other adaptations.

title: The Lost Footage of Leah Sullivan
Black Mask
rt_critics_consensus: Stylish setting and masterfully choreographed fight scenes, but the story is low on credibility and high on excessive violence and gore.

title: The Legend of the 7 Golden Vampires
Scream 2
rt_critics_consensus: As with the first film, Scream 2 is a gleeful takedown of scary movie conventions that manages to poke fun at terrible horror sequels without falling victim to the same fate.

title: Jane
rt_critics_consensus: Jane honors its subject's legacy with an absorbing, beautifully filmed, and overall enlightening look at her decades of invaluable work.

title: Filth to Ashes, Flesh to Dust
Cuties
rt_critics_consensus: A thoughtful look at the intricacies of girlhood in the modern age, Cuties is a coming-of-age film that confronts its themes with poignancy and nuance.

title: We Are the Flesh
rt_critics_consensus: Visually striking and aggressively confrontational, We are the Flesh may prove as difficult to watch as it ultimately is to forget.

title: The Hammer of Witches: The Screech Owl
Detour
rt_critics_consensus: Stylish and gripping, Detour offers further proof that a patsy and a femme fatale often add up to a satisfying story.

title: Bad Samaritan
rt_critics_consensus: Bad Samaritan delivers a handful of genre thrills -- and a stellar central performance by David Tennant -- that help offset iffy direction and a disappointingly mundane story.

title: Zombieland: Double Tap
rt_critics_consensus: Zombieland: Double Tap makes up for a lack of fresh brains with an enjoyable reunion that recaptures the spirit of the original and adds a few fun twists.

title: Winchester
rt_critics_consensus: Like a grand staircase within the famous mansion that inspired it, Winchester appears poised to get a rise out of audiences, but ultimately leads nowhere.

title: Make Me Shudder 2: Shudder Me Mae Nak
Head Count
rt_critics_consensus: Head Count doesn't rack up enough punchy scares to be an unqualified success as a horror outing, but an engaging cast and textured characters make it a refreshing genre exercise with something on its mind.

title: The Beta Test
rt_critics_consensus: A darkly amusing thriller that discomfits as it entertains, The Beta Test satirizes Hollywood with savage flair.

title: Wolf Creek 2
rt_critics_consensus: After a strong start, Wolf Creek 2 devolves into an unnecessary -- and disappointingly predictable -- sequel.

title: The Skin I Live In
rt_critics_consensus: The Skin I Live In lacks Almodovar's famously charged romance, replaced with a wonderfully bizarre and unpredictable detour into arthouse ick.

title: Do You See What I See
Nobody Sleeps in the Woods Tonight 2
The Vanishing
rt_critics_consensus: A clinical, maddening descent into the mind of a serial killer and a slowly unraveling hero, culminating with one of the scariest endings of all time.

title: Perpetrator
rt_critics_consensus: A smartly ambitious supernatural thriller, Perpetrator blends an assortment of genres and influences into a bloody good time.

title: Death Wish
rt_critics_consensus: Death Wish is undeniably exploitation fare -- and also undeniably effective.

title: Be My Cat: A Film for Anne
Billy the Kid and the Green Baize Vampire
Pay the Ghost
rt_critics_consensus: Pay the Ghost takes a weak stab at supernatural horror, but ultimately, it's only viewers who will pay for watching this sloppily assembled picture.

title: The Djinn
rt_critics_consensus: Led by Ezra Dewey's standout performance, The Djinn serves up a scary, sleekly effective cautionary tale about being careful what you wish for.

title: The Apparition
rt_critics_consensus: The Apparition fails to offer anything original, isn't particularly scary, and offers so little in the way of dramatic momentum that it's more likely to put you to sleep than thrill you.

title: The Hunchback of Notre Dame
rt_critics_consensus: Disney's take on the Victor Hugo classic is dramatically uneven, but its strong visuals, dark themes, and message of tolerance make for a more-sophisticated-than-average children's film.

title: Climax
rt_critics_consensus: Challenging and rewarding in equal measure, Climax captures writer-director Gaspar Noé working near his technically brilliant and visually distinctive peak.

title: The House Was Not Hungry Then
Darkness
rt_critics_consensus: Yet another predictable variation on the hoary old haunted-house movie, Darkness is an illogical, portentous mess.

title: Assassinio al cimitero etrusco (Scorpion with Two Tails) (Murder in an Etruscan Cemetery)
The Strangers
rt_critics_consensus: The Strangers has a handful of genuinely scary moments, but they're not enough to elevate the end results above standard slasher fare.

title: Das Vorspiel
rt_critics_consensus: It may occasionally veer into discordant territory, but The Audition remains a darkly illuminating character study led by a forceful turn from Nina Hoss.

title: Boogeyman
rt_critics_consensus: The plot is been-there done-that generic, and none of the shock effects can do anything to build up suspense.

title: The Fog
rt_critics_consensus: The Fog is a so-so remake of a so-so movie, lacking scares, suspense or originality.

title: Paranormal Activity
rt_critics_consensus: Using its low-budget effects and mockumentary method to great result, Paranormal Activity turns a simple haunted house story into 90 minutes of relentless suspense.

title: Sometimes Aunt Martha Does Dreadful Things
Bewitched
rt_critics_consensus: Bewitched is haunted by scattered laughs and a lack of direction.

title: A Haunted House 2
rt_critics_consensus: Sloppy, vulgar, and manic, A Haunted House 2 might be worth a chuckle or two, but mostly it's a string of pop culture references and crude gags that fail to hit their intended targets.

title: Smile 2
rt_critics_consensus: Blessed with a nerve-jangling star turn by Naomi Scott, writer-director Parker Finn broadens Smile's conceit into a pop stardom nightmare that'll leave a rictus grin on horror fans' faces.

title: Elf
rt_critics_consensus: A movie full of Yuletide cheer, Elf is a spirited, good-natured family comedy, and it benefits greatly from Will Ferrell's funny and charming performance as one of Santa's biggest helpers.

title: Halloween: The Curse of Michael Myers
rt_critics_consensus: Halloween: The Curse of Michael Myers trades the simple, brutal effectiveness of the original for convoluted mysticism, with disastrously dull results.

title: Bitch Ass
rt_critics_consensus: It's more entertaining in theory than execution, but if you're looking for an amusingly cheesy slasher, Bitch Ass should just about fit the bill.

title: Primeval
rt_critics_consensus: Primeval is a low-quality horror film, which due to the inane political messages does not even qualify as campy fun.

title: Queens vs Zombies from Outer Space
The Wanderers: The Quest of The Demon Hunter
Resident Evil: Extinction
rt_critics_consensus: Resident Evil: Extinction is more of the same; its few impressive action sequences unable to compensate for the pedestrian plot.

title: Around the Bend
rt_critics_consensus: Around the Bend sets a destination of earnest generational drama, but settles for the most derivative route to get there, veering off course into trite sentimentality.

title: The Nightmare
rt_critics_consensus: Part documentary, part thriller, The Nightmare works just well enough in both respects to deliver a uniquely disturbing viewing experience.

title: Brotherhood of the Wolf
rt_critics_consensus: Brotherhood of the Wolf mixes its genres with little logic, but the end result is wildly entertaining.

title: Into the Forest
rt_critics_consensus: Into the Forest grounds its familiar apocalyptic framework with a relatable look at the bond between two sisters, compellingly brought to life by Elliot Page and Evan Rachel Wood.

title: Vicious Fun
rt_critics_consensus: A fiendishly goofy premise, strong performances, and a strong dose of winkingly meta humor help Vicious Fun deliver on its title.

title: Baby Ruby
rt_critics_consensus: A flawed but finely cut gem, Baby Ruby puts the horror of new parenthood under a frighteningly effective magnifying glass.

title: The Grudge
rt_critics_consensus: There's some creepy imagery to be found, but not much in the way of logic or truly jarring scares.

title: Single Black Female 3: The Final Chapter
Unsane
rt_critics_consensus: Unsane unleashes Steven Soderbergh's inner B-movie maestro, wading into timeless psychological thriller territory and giving it a high-tech filmmaking spin.

title: Shutter
rt_critics_consensus: You just might Shutter in fear with this new-fangled Japanese horror film, but its approach is all too formulaic to leave a lasting impression.

title: All My Friends Hate Me
rt_critics_consensus: As discomfiting as it is hard to turn away from, All My Friends Hate Me taps into the humor -- and horror -- of awkward social situations.

title: Suspended Animation
rt_critics_consensus: Suspended Animation offers some gory good fun for genre fans, even if the end results don't quite transcend their cheerfully shallow roots.

title: Bedtime Stories: Tales of Love and Hate
Found Footage: The Making of The Patterson Project
Dead Snow: Red vs. Dead
rt_critics_consensus: Dead Snow 2: Red vs. Dead expands the original's canvas without sacrificing any of its bloody fun, adding up to a sequel that fans of the first are bound to enjoy.

title: The Doors
rt_critics_consensus: Val Kilmer delivers a powerhouse performance as one of rock's most incendiary figures, but unfortunately, Oliver Stone is unable to shed much light on the circus surrounding the star.

title: Child's Play
rt_critics_consensus: Child's Play occasionally stumbles across its tonal tightrope of comedy and horror, but its genuinely creepy monster and some deft direction by Tom Holland makes this chiller stand out on the shelf.

title: I Didn't Come Here to Die
Five Nights at Freddy's
rt_critics_consensus: Loaded with Easter eggs, Five Nights at Freddy's may be fun to watch for fans of the game, but most viewers of any other persuasion will find this adaptation muddled and decidedly unscary.

title: Ang Mahiwagang Daigdig ni Pedro Penduko
Day Watch
rt_critics_consensus: Day Watch is frequently cheesy but it offers enough twists, surprises, and inventive action sequences to maintain viewer interest.

title: End of Days
rt_critics_consensus: An overblown thriller with formulaic action scenes and poor acting.

title: Tucker & Dale vs Evil
rt_critics_consensus: Like the best horror/comedies, Tucker & Dale vs. Evil mines its central crazy joke for some incredible scares, laughs, and -- believe it or not -- heart.

title: She Was So Pretty: Be Good for Goodness Sake
Nazis at the Center of the Earth
Humanist Vampire Seeking Consenting Suicidal Person
rt_critics_consensus: While not as innovative as its title, Humanist Vampire Seeking Consenting Suicidal Person offers a dark-ish comedic take on a teen love story between misfits that renders a strangely sweet bite.

title: Critters
rt_critics_consensus: While Critters ekes out some fun from a game cast and screwball tone, the titular monsters fail to deliver the credible menace that makes a creature feature satisfying.

title: Welcome to the Occult Forest: The Movie
Beba
rt_critics_consensus: Beba's narrow point of view limits its impact to an extent, but this remains an impressive work of introspective documentary filmmaking.

title: Reindeer Games
rt_critics_consensus: Despite a decent cast, subpar acting and a contrived plot disappointed reviewers.

title: Texas Zombie Wars: El Paso Outpost
Broken Lizard's Club Dread
rt_critics_consensus: Comedy is too hit-or-miss in this slasher spoof.

title: Camp
rt_critics_consensus: Campy comedy that squeaks by on its charms.

title: A Nightmare on Elm Street 4: The Dream Master
rt_critics_consensus: A Nightmare on Elm Street 4: The Dream Master marks a relative high point in this franchise's bumpy creative journey, although the original remains far superior.

title: Scare Package
rt_critics_consensus: Some of its contents are more entertaining than others, but genre fans should still find this Scare Package well worth opening.

title: The Changeling
rt_critics_consensus: George C. Scott's somber performance gives this haunted house horror a moving soul to go along with its harrowing scares.

title: House of 1000 Corpses
rt_critics_consensus: There's an abundance of gore in this derivative horror movie, but little sense or wit.

title: (Untitled)
rt_critics_consensus: This satire on the art world is at times both clever and shallow, but its top-notch cast generates plenty of goodwill.

title: Vivarium
rt_critics_consensus: Vivarium may confound almost as often as it intrigues, but this well-acted sci-fi/horror hybrid has interesting ideas -- and explores them with style.
Loosely based on 1957's 12 Angry Men, Nikita Mikhalkov's superbly acted 12 is clever and gripping like its predecessor, but with a distinctly Russian feel.

title: Maelstrom
rt_critics_consensus: A peculiar breach of morality that leads with pretension: Denis Villeneuve guides Maelstrom through teetering absurdity without malicious intent.

title: Giant God Warrior Appears in Tokyo
What Keeps You Alive
rt_critics_consensus: Smart, stylish, and well-acted, What Keeps You Alive proves it's still possible to spin an engrossing horror yarn without fundamentally altering established formula.

title: Triumph Over Evil: Battle of the Exorcists
Sharknado
rt_critics_consensus: Proudly, shamelessly, and gloriously brainless, Sharknado redefines "so bad it's good" for a new generation.

title: Algo que Pasó en Año Nuevo
Primal
rt_critics_consensus: Chiefly of interest to Nicolas Cage completists and hardcore B-movie fans, this action thriller suffers from an unfortunate lack of Primal energy.

title: Man of the House
rt_critics_consensus: A high-concept movie that plays out like a mediocre TV sitcom.

title: Neither the Sea nor the Sand
Battle Girl: The Living Dead in Tokyo Bay
The Bowery Boys Meet the Monsters
Murder Mystery
rt_critics_consensus: Murder Mystery reunites Jennifer Aniston and Adam Sandler for a lightweight comedy that's content to settle for merely mediocre.

title: Annabelle Comes Home
rt_critics_consensus: Fun for fans even if it isn't as frightening as some of its predecessors, Annabelle Comes Home suggests there's still some life left in the Conjuring franchise.

title: Trash Fire
rt_critics_consensus: Trash Fire has a confidently scabrous appeal, although its unsympathetic characters and nihilistic black comedy may leave a sour taste with some viewers.

title: Bad Taste
rt_critics_consensus: Peter Jackson's early low-budget shocker boasts a disgusting premise - aliens harvesting humans for fast food - that gives the budding auteur plenty of room for gross-out visuals and absurd cleverness.

title: A Girl Walks Home Alone at Night
rt_critics_consensus: A Girl Walks Home Alone at Night blends conventional elements into something brilliantly original -- and serves as a striking calling card for writer-director Ana Lily Amirpour.

title: A Desert
rt_critics_consensus: A metatextual meditation on cinema made with inventive style, A Desert's cerebral frights are enough to satisfy anyone parched for thoughtful horror.

title: Below
rt_critics_consensus: Below is a creepy, claustrophobic exercise in style.

title: Himalaya
rt_critics_consensus: Unlike the overproduced and overbudgeted Hollywood blockbusters, Himalaya is a stunningly photographed movie that offers an intimate look into the lives of a Dolpo tribe.

title: Resident Evil: Welcome to Raccoon City
rt_critics_consensus: Resident Evil: Welcome to Raccoon City is an affectionately faithful adaptation that further proves its source material is ill-suited to the big screen.

title: There's Something in the Barn
rt_critics_consensus: There's Something in the Barn unwraps a wildly entertaining holiday rampage filled with witty jokes, creature feature charm, and eye-popping practical effects.

title: Into the Night
rt_critics_consensus: Despite its two stellar leads, Into the Night finds director John Landis indulging in far too many gimmicks in lieu of a well-rounded story.

title: The Relic
rt_critics_consensus: Fleeting moments of brainless monster mayhem aside, The Relic proves to be too predictably derivative for its own good.

title: Trilogy of Terror
rt_critics_consensus: Trilogy of Terror delivers a twisty anthology of macabre stories driven by Karen Black's captivating multi-character performance and whirlwind visuals.

title: Awake
rt_critics_consensus: Awake has an interesting premise but would have benefited from tighter performances and more efficient direction and editing.

title: Frankie Freako
rt_critics_consensus: A gleefully silly, puppet-filled homage to '80s creature features, Frankie Freako fully embraces its own absurdity to supply a brisk and irreverent dose of horror-comedy fun.

title: The Gallows
rt_critics_consensus: Narratively contrived and visually a mess, The Gallows sends viewers on a shaky tumble to the bottom of the found-footage horror barrel.

title: Freaks
rt_critics_consensus: Time has been kind to this horror legend: Freaks manages to frighten, shock, and even touch viewers in ways that contemporary viewers missed.

title: Slither
rt_critics_consensus: A slimy, B-movie homage oozing with affection for low-budget horror films, Slither is creepy and funny -- if you've got the stomach for it.

title: Final Destination 3
rt_critics_consensus: Final Destination 3 is more of the same: gory and pointless, with nowhere new to go.

title: Umma
rt_critics_consensus: Despite Sandra Oh's typically strong performance and glimmers of potential from writer-director Iris K. Shim, Umma is a cliché-ridden missed opportunity.

title: Mikadroid - Robokill Beneath Disco Club Layla
Scream 4
rt_critics_consensus: The franchise is showing its age, but Scream 4 is undeniably an improvement over its predecessor, with just enough meta humor and clever kills.

title: Mainstream
rt_critics_consensus: Mainstream makes a vain attempt to satirize viral fame, settling instead for obvious commentary that feels painfully dated.

title: Pine Bros. Presents: Cinema Insomnia Haunted House Special
Bones
rt_critics_consensus: Slow to start, the sleek looking Bones is more silly than scary.

title: Blood Red Sky
rt_critics_consensus: Blood Red Sky makes the most of its high-concept vampire story, delivering a fast-paced treat for genre enthusiasts.

title: The Curse of the Mummy's Tomb
Wishmaster
rt_critics_consensus: Wishmaster searches for horror in the exploits of a supernatural being -- one whose powers, alas, evidently do not include the ability to summon a compelling script.

title: Magic
rt_critics_consensus: Thanks in large part to Anthony Hopkins' layered performance, Magic is an unusual albeit creepily effective experience.

title: Prometheus Triumphant: A Fugue in the Key of Flesh
The Disappointments Room
rt_critics_consensus: The Disappointments Room lives down to its title with a thrill-free thriller that presumably left its stars filled with regret - and threatens to do the same for audiences.

title: Evil Breed: The Legend of Samhain
I Spit Chew on Your Grave
Stir of Echoes
rt_critics_consensus: Kevin Bacon's acting is so genuine that it's creepy and director David Keopp knows how to create true suspense.

title: Birth
rt_critics_consensus: A well-mounted production is undermined by a muddled, absurd storyline of questionable taste.

title: Bookworm
rt_critics_consensus: Recapturing the magic of vintage adventure yarns, Bookworm is an imaginative gem with a wholesome heart.

title: For a Good Time, Call ...
rt_critics_consensus: Brisk, funny, and sweetly raunchy, For a Good Time, Call... adds to the recent string of R-rated female comedies while serving as an overdue coming out party for the charming Ari Graynor.

title: Project Wolf Hunting
rt_critics_consensus: Project Wolf Hunting's slight story takes a back seat to all the action -- and for fans of all-out cinematic mayhem, that's exactly as it should be.

title: Blood Reign: Curse of the Yoma
Jack Brooks: Monster Slayer
rt_critics_consensus: A sincere but sometimes-excessive send up of low-rent horror hokum, Jack Brooks: Monster Slayer features some sharp laughs and features a scene-stealing performance by Robert Englund.

title: Saltwater: The Battle for Ramree Island
The Ugly Stepsister
rt_critics_consensus: Taking a hammer and chisel to a quintessential fairy tale, The Ugly Stepsister's masterful application of gore and subversion are the stuff that nightmares are made of.

title: I Saw the TV Glow
rt_critics_consensus: With a distinctive visual aesthetic that enhances its emotionally resonant narrative, I Saw the TV Glow further establishes writer-director Jane Schoenbrun as a rising talent.

title: Nightbitch
rt_critics_consensus: As far as adaptations go, Nightbitch packs more bark than bite as the stellar Amy Adams takes on motherhood and the neighborhood in a rather conventional romp.

title: Strange Darling
rt_critics_consensus: JT Mollner delivers a thrillingly unexpected and electric ride with two breakout performances by Willa Fitzgerald and Kyle Gallner in Strange Darling.

title: Dark Glasses
rt_critics_consensus: While it's far from Dario Argento's best, Dark Glasses may be worth a look for fans hungry for another horrific helping from a master of his craft.

title: Let the Corpses Tan
rt_critics_consensus: Let the Corpses Tan challenges the audience's expectations -- and delivers a singularly stylish, unforgettably unique viewing experience in the bargain.

title: The Treatment
rt_critics_consensus: A comedy for grown-ups, The Treatment overcomes some technical lapses by virtue of a sharp cast.

title: Closet Monster
rt_critics_consensus: Closet Monster is a unique, understated fable, buoyed by a strong performance from Connor Jessup.

title: Child of Darkness, Child of Light
Grindhouse
rt_critics_consensus: Grindhouse delivers exhilarating exploitation fare with wit and panache, improving upon its source material with feral intelligence.

title: Rabid
rt_critics_consensus: Its gruesome scares may nauseate more than thrill, but Rabid bites into its story with distinct sophistication and thought-provoking themes.

title: The Blob
rt_critics_consensus: In spite of its chortle-worthy premise and dated special effects, The Blob remains a prime example of how satisfying cheesy B-movie monster thrills can be.

title: Playtime
rt_critics_consensus: A remarkable achievement, Playtime's packs every scene with sight gags and characters that both celebrates and satirizes the urbanization of modern life.

title: O
rt_critics_consensus: Though well-intentioned and serious in its exploration of teen violence, O is an uneven experiment that doesn't quite succeed.

title: The Cut
rt_critics_consensus: Ambitious and well-intentioned, The Cut is visually arresting and at times, even emotionally piercing, but ultimately fails to transcend.

title: House at the End of the Street
rt_critics_consensus: Poorly conceived, clumsily executed, and almost completely bereft of scares, House at the End of the Street strands its talented star in a film as bland as its title.

title: Wendigo
rt_critics_consensus: An artsy horror flick, Wendigo effectively creates an eerie atmosphere.

title: In Search of Darkness: A Journey Into Iconic 80s Horror
Christmas Bloody Christmas
rt_critics_consensus: Perhaps it isn't quite as much fun as a movie about a murderous robot Santa ought to be, but for fans of holiday horror, Christmas Bloody Christmas is still a gift.

title: In Search of Darkness: Part III
47 Meters Down: Uncaged
rt_critics_consensus: 47 Meters Down: Uncaged may not be as ruthlessly efficient as its finned villains, but fans of shark peril thrillers should find it just chummy enough.

title: The Boy Who Killed My Parents
Beowulf
rt_critics_consensus: Featuring groundbreaking animation, stunning visuals, and a talented cast, Beowulf has in spades what more faithful book adaptations forget to bring: pure cinematic entertainment.

title: Ang Mahiwagang Daigdig ni Elias Paniki
Come Out, Come Out, Wherever You Are
We're All Going to the World's Fair
rt_critics_consensus: Narratively challenging and visually haunting, We're All Going to the World's Fair adds a uniquely ambitious and unsettling entry to the crowded coming-of-age genre.

title: Midnight Movies: From the Margin to the Mainstream
The Haunting of Fox Hollow Farm
Skinamarink
rt_critics_consensus: Skinamarink can be more confounding than frightening, but for viewers able or willing to dial into its unique wavelength, this unsettling film will be difficult to shake.

title: A Ghost Story
rt_critics_consensus: A Ghost Story deftly manages its ambitious themes through an inventive, artful, and ultimately poignant exploration of love and loss.

title: Mimic
rt_critics_consensus: Mimic finds director Guillermo del Toro struggling to inject his unique sensibilities into a studio picture - and delivering just enough genre thrills to recommend.

title: Q
rt_critics_consensus: Q's campy charms may be lost on audiences who want their monsters frightening, but a game cast and lovingly retrograde visual effects give this kaiju romp some majesty.

title: Sleeper
rt_critics_consensus: In Sleeper, Woody Allen's madcap futurist comedy, practically each joke and one-liner hits it target.

title: Dark Water
rt_critics_consensus: All the atmospherics in Dark Water can't make up for the lack of genuine scares.

title: One Cut of the Dead Spin-Off: In Hollywood
Wolf Creek
rt_critics_consensus: Though Wolf Creek is effectively horrific, it is still tasteless exploitation.

title: Doctor Sleep
rt_critics_consensus: Doctor Sleep forsakes the elemental terror of its predecessor for a more contemplative sequel that balances poignant themes against spine-tingling chills.

title: Where the Truth Lies
rt_critics_consensus: The belabored noir plotting feels unbelievable, thus removing any sense of suspense. Also, Lohman is badly miscast.

title: Urban Nightmares: A Found Footage Anthology
Hell House LLC: Lineage
rt_critics_consensus: Recycling convoluted mythology, lethargic plotting, and flat characters into an overlong slog, Hell House LLC: Lineage limps to an exasperating and scare-deficient finale.

title: Krampus
rt_critics_consensus: Krampus is gory good fun for fans of non-traditional holiday horror with a fondness for Joe Dante's B- movie classics, even if it doesn't have quite the savage bite its concept calls for.

title: The Rare Blue Apes of Cannibal Isle
All the Gods in the Sky
Titane
rt_critics_consensus: Thrillingly provocative and original, Titane reaffirms writer-director Julia Ducournau's delightfully disturbing vision.

title: John Dies at the End
rt_critics_consensus: Some will find the darkly funny, genre-bending incoherence of John Dies at the End charming; some will feel its zany antics and gore lead to an unsatisfying payoff.

title: Scream, Queen! My Nightmare on Elm Street
rt_critics_consensus: Scream, Queen! My Nightmare on Elm Street compassionately draws on an actor's personal journey to offer a compelling and illuminating reexamination of the franchise's first sequel.

title: Nightmares in Red, White and Blue
He Loves Me... He Loves Me Not
rt_critics_consensus: This romantic thriller puts Tautou's sweetheart Amelie image to good use.

title: Cam
rt_critics_consensus: Smart and suspenseful, CAM is a techno-thriller that's far more than the sum of its salacious parts -- and an outstanding showcase for Madeline Brewer in the leading role.

title: Slender Man
rt_critics_consensus: Slender Man might be thin, but he's positively robust compared to the flimsy assortment of scares generated by the would-be chiller that bears his name.

title: Oh Canada
rt_critics_consensus: Anchored by Richard Gere's egoless performance, Oh Canada is a thorny and sometimes muddled memory play that fits solidly into director Paul Schrader's self-reflective filmography.

title: birth/rebirth
rt_critics_consensus: Firmly rooted in classic horror, Birth/Rebirth uses a familiar framework to tease at fundamental fears from a chillingly relatable angle.

title: What Really Happened to Baby Jane
Lesbian Vampire Killers
rt_critics_consensus: Lesbian Vampire Killers stakes a claim to niche British humor, but ultimately succumbs to dreary twaddle.

title: And Here No Devil Can Hurt You
Hatching
rt_critics_consensus: A message movie in a hard horror shell, Hatching perches between beauty and revulsion -- and establishes director Hanna Bergholm as a bright new talent.

title: Session 9
rt_critics_consensus: Relying more on atmosphere than gore, Session 9 is effectively creepy.

title: Unfriended: Dark Web
rt_critics_consensus: Unfriended: Dark Web is more interested in chills than an exploration of its timely themes, but horror fans should still find this sequel to be steadily, undeniably effective.

title: Idol of Evil: Hell Is Forever
It Doesn't Get Any Better Than This
Hold Your Breath
rt_critics_consensus: Sarah Paulson is as arresting as ever, but a dearth of incident and scares ultimately robs Hold Your Breath of long-awaited release.

title: The Monkey
rt_critics_consensus: Cruelly clever with some unforgettably gory set pieces, The Monkey reaffirms director Osgood Perkins' horror bona fides while revealing he also has a surprising -- albeit sick -- sense of humor.

title: Living with Chucky
rt_critics_consensus: While it may lack the twisted pizzazz of the franchise it chronicles, Living with Chucky is a mostly engaging primer on everyone's favorite homicidal plaything.

title: Jason X
rt_critics_consensus: Jason goes to the future, but the story is still stuck in the past.

title: Azrael
rt_critics_consensus: Lead by another bloody fantastic performance from Samara Weaving, Azrael sacrifices depth on the altar of storytelling economy but delivers pure adrenaline in the bargain.

title: Family Portraits: A Trilogy of America
Halloween II
rt_critics_consensus: Halloween II picks up where its predecessor left off and quickly wanders into a dead end that the franchise would spend decades struggling to find its way out of.

title: The Substance
rt_critics_consensus: Audaciously gross, wickedly clever, and possibly Demi Moore's finest hour, The Substance is a gasp-inducing feat from writer-director Coralie Fargeat.

title: Dirty Love
rt_critics_consensus: The laugh-free Dirty Love is a comedy dead zone -- it's aggressively crude and shoddily constructed.

title: Benediction
rt_critics_consensus: It isn't an easy watch, but Benediction uncovers a profoundly affecting drama in the real-life story of a combat veteran whose poetry warned against the horrors of war.

title: Final Days: Tales From The End Times
Knock Off
rt_critics_consensus: Muddled plot; stiff acting.

title: The Columnist
rt_critics_consensus: Savagely funny and viscerally unsettling, The Columnist takes the unbridled vitriol of social media to its bloodily over-the-top conclusion.

title: Alien Trespass
rt_critics_consensus: An earnest attempt to parody campy 1950s sci-fi films, Alien Trespass eventually loses its charm among tedious dialogue and cheesy special effects.

title: Godzilla
rt_critics_consensus: Without compelling characters or heart, Godzilla stomps on everything that made the original (or any monster movie worth its salt) a classic.

title: Apartment 7A
rt_critics_consensus: Apartment 7A is slickly assembled and solidly acted, but the lingering memory of Rosemary's Baby and a predictable prequel template make for a sub(par)lease.

title: The Roommate
rt_critics_consensus: Devoid of chills, thrills, or even cheap titillation, The Roommate isn't even bad enough to be good.

title: Possession
rt_critics_consensus: It's perhaps a bit tame and uninspiring, considering its subject matter, but Possession manages enough romance and period intrigue to satisfy most fans of its source material.

title: Dust Bunny
rt_critics_consensus: Elevated by Mads Mikkelsen and Sophie Sloan's magnetic chemistry, Dust Bunny is a dazzlingly imaginative and stylish feature debut from director Bryan Fuller.

title: Stigmata
rt_critics_consensus: The story is unconvincing and the acting is weak.

title: The Wolf of Snow Hollow
rt_critics_consensus: The Wolf of Snow Hollow treads somewhat unsteadily between horror and comedy, but writer-director-star Jim Cummings' unique sensibilities make for an oddly haunting hybrid.

title: Trouble Every Day
rt_critics_consensus: An erotic thriller dulled by a messy narrative.

title: Bundy and the Green River Killer
The Snapper
rt_critics_consensus: Warm, funny, and populated by easily relatable characters, The Snapper is a compulsively watchable slice-of-life dramedy with much to recommend.

title: The Verdict
rt_critics_consensus: Paul Newman is at the peak of his powers as an attorney who never lived up to his potential in The Verdict, supported by David Mamet's crackling script and Sidney Lumet's confident direction.

title: Fever Dream
rt_critics_consensus: The meaning of this alluring drama can feel as elusive as a Fever Dream, but it's steadily absorbing and consistently difficult to look away.

title: Incident at Loch Ness
rt_critics_consensus: Incident at Loch Ness will undoubtedly be of greatest interest to Herzog fans, but for those in on the inside jokes, it delivers on its weirdly unique premise.

title: Housebound
rt_critics_consensus: Alternately hilarious, gross, and simply diverting, Housebound is the rare horror-comedy that delivers on both fronts.

title: The Man Who Sold the World
Late Night with the Devil
rt_critics_consensus: Delightfully dark, Late Night with the Devil proves possession horror isn't played out -- and serves as an outstanding showcase for David Dastmalchian.

title: The Child Who Would Not Come
Labyrinth
rt_critics_consensus: While it's arguably more interesting on a visual level, Labyrinth provides further proof of director Jim Henson's boundless imagination.

title: Dead Snow
rt_critics_consensus: Though it doesn't cover new ground, Dead Snow is an entertaining mix of camp, scares, and blood and guts.

title: The Place Where the Last Man Died
Home Alone
rt_critics_consensus: Home Alone's uneven but frequently funny premise, stretched unreasonably thin, is buoyed by Macaulay Culkin's cute performance and strong supporting stars.

title: I Still Know What You Did Last Summer
rt_critics_consensus: Boring, predictable, and bereft of thrills or chills, I Still Know What You Did Last Summer is exactly the kind of rehash that gives horror sequels a bad name.

title: Lake Placid
rt_critics_consensus: Betty White's delightful supporting turn may be worth the price of admission alone, but Lake Placid is swamped by a smarmy script and inability to deliver on the creature feature mayhem.

title: P2
rt_critics_consensus: P2 is full of gore, but low on suspense, featuring a cat-and-mouse plot has been done many times before.

title: Went Up the Hill
rt_critics_consensus: Heavy on mood and light on answers, Went Up the Hill is a heady ghost story that weaves an intoxicating spell for those patient enough to surrender to its languid rhythms.

title: Medusa
rt_critics_consensus: A stylish and well-acted tonal blend, Medusa confronts religious hypocrisy and sexism with invigorating anger.

title: The Ghost Club: Spirits Never Die
The Red Book Ritual: Gates of Hell
Hell Fest
rt_critics_consensus: Hell Fest might give less demanding horror fans a few decent reasons to scream, but it's neither clever nor frightening enough to leave much of an impression.

title: Amish Witches: The True Story of Holmes County
London After Midnight
rt_critics_consensus: Lon Chaney's typically strong performance -- and terrifying makeup -- casts an eerie spell in this solid detective thriller.

title: Wolves Against the World (Fresh Hell Presents)
The Kingdom
rt_critics_consensus: While providing several top-notch action scenes, The Kingdom ultimately collapses under the weight of formula and muddled politics.

title: The Final Girls
rt_critics_consensus: The Final Girls offers an affectionate nod to slasher tropes while adding a surprising layer of genuine emotion to go with the meta amusement.

title: A House Is Not a Home
Sanctum
rt_critics_consensus: Sanctum is beautifully photographed, and it makes better use of 3-D technology than most, but that doesn't make up for its ham-handed script and lifeless cast.

title: Kaizoku Sentai Gokaiger the Movie: The Flying Ghost Ship
Fantasia
rt_critics_consensus: A landmark in animation (and a huge influence on the medium of music video), Disney's Fantasia is a relentlessly inventive blend of the classics with phantasmagorical images.

title: The Heat
rt_critics_consensus: The Heat is predictable, but Melissa McCarthy is reliably funny and Sandra Bullock proves a capable foil.

title: Persona
rt_critics_consensus: Arguably Bergman's finest film, Persona explores the human condition with intense curiosity, immense technical skill, and beguiling warmth.

title: What Lies Beneath
rt_critics_consensus: Robert Zemeckis is unable to salvage an uncompelling and unoriginal film.

title: The Short Night of the Glass Dolls
Blair Witch
rt_critics_consensus: Blair Witch doles out a handful of effective scares, but aside from a few new twists, it mainly offers a belated rehash of the original -- and far more memorable -- first film.

title: On a Dark and Bloody Ground
The Unforeseen
rt_critics_consensus: A calm documentary that doesn't resort to shrill polemics, The Unforeseen also benefits from great interview subjects and jaw-dropping cinematography.

title: The Covenant
rt_critics_consensus: The Covenant plays out like a teen soap opera, full of pretty faces, wooden acting, laughable dialogue, and little suspense.

title: Happy Death Day
rt_critics_consensus: Happy Death Day puts a darkly humorous sci-fi spin on slasher conventions, with added edge courtesy of a starmaking performance from Jessica Rothe.

title: Fried Barry
rt_critics_consensus: A bizarrely memorable sci-fi/comedy hybrid, Fried Barry may be an acquired taste, but it certainly isn't chicken.

title: The Last House on Cemetery Lane
They Remain
rt_critics_consensus: They Remain relies on rich atmosphere to effectively establish a sense of creeping dread that's almost enough to cover for its nagging narrative deficiencies.

title: The Object of My Affection
rt_critics_consensus: Despite heartfelt performances from Jennifer Aniston and Paul Rudd, The Object of My Affection suffers from too many plot contrivances and frequent turns into rom-com sappiness.

title: Lockjaw: Rise of the Kulev Serpent
Kwaidan
rt_critics_consensus: Exquisitely designed and fastidiously ornate, Masaki Kobayashi's ambitious anthology operates less as a frightening example of horror and more as a meditative tribute to Japanese folklore.

title: Cyrus: Mind of a Serial Killer
Out of the Past
rt_critics_consensus: Anchored by a wistful Robert Mitchum, Out of the Past is an exemplary noir steeped in doom and sensuality.

title: It: Chapter Two
rt_critics_consensus: It: Chapter Two proves bigger doesn't always mean scarier for horror sequels, but a fine cast and faithful approach to the source material keep this follow-up afloat.

title: Chuck Steel: Night of the Trampires
In My Mother's Skin
rt_critics_consensus: Grim and macabre, In My Mother's Skin is a bleakly effective blend of historical and supernatural horror.

title: The Ruins
rt_critics_consensus: Despite a solid cast and truly frightening source material, The Ruins founders, thanks to a weak script and an excess of gore.

title: Therapy for a Vampire
rt_critics_consensus: Therapy for a Vampire should get a few chuckles out of vampire movie fans in search of something new, even if it never quite lives up to its daffy potential.

title: A Serbian Film
rt_critics_consensus: A pointless shocker and societal allegory, a film whose imagery is so gruesome as to leave you scarred for life...or rolling your eyes for 100 minutes.

title: Nothing Bad Can Happen
rt_critics_consensus: Nothing Bad Can Happen uses its protagonist's punishing ordeal to pose questions about faith that are well worth considering, even if the film can't quite answer them.

title: Red
rt_critics_consensus: It may not be the killer thrill ride you'd expect from an action movie with a cast of this caliber, but Red still thoroughly outshines most of its big-budget counterparts with its wit and style.

title: Brahms: The Boy II
rt_critics_consensus: More likely to induce boredom than quicken the pulse, Brahms: The Boy II is chiefly scary for the way it undermines the effectiveness of its above-average predecessor.

title: You're Not Supposed to Be Here
White Noise
rt_critics_consensus: While there are some built-in scares, the movie is muddled and unsatisfying.

title: Users
rt_critics_consensus: There's no quibbling with its ambitious aims, but Users is more repetitive and less intelligent than it seems to think.

title: Sleepaway Camp
rt_critics_consensus: Sleepaway Camp is a standard teen slasher elevated by occasional moments of John Waters-esque weirdness and a twisted ending.

title: The Huntress: Rune of the Dead
Snatchers
rt_critics_consensus: Snatchers' practical effects and strong writing come together to create a sci-fi comedy that's fun, frenetic, and at times, freakin' crazy (in the best way).

title: Lifeforce
rt_critics_consensus: Brazenly strange and uneven in its execution, Lifeforce is an otherworldly sci-fi excursion punctuated with off-kilter horror flourishes.

title: Bodycam
rt_critics_consensus: With smart, low budget ingenuity and visceral atmosphere, Bodycam delivers a brisk and often gripping found footage experience.

title: The Man in the Orange Jacket
Status Quo? The Unfinished Business of Feminism in Canada
Onyx the Fortuitous and the Talisman of Souls
Peter Pan's Neverland Nightmare
rt_critics_consensus: Continuing the trend of schlocky puerile remakes, Peter Pan's Neverland Nightmare guts a beloved character for low-brow horror that never manages to take flight.

title: Event Horizon
rt_critics_consensus: Despite a strong opening that promises sci-fi thrills, Event Horizon quickly devolves into an exercise of style over substance whose flashy effects and gratuitous gore fail to mask its overreliance on horror clichés.

title: Frenzy
rt_critics_consensus: Marking Alfred Hitchcock's return to England and first foray into viscerally explicit carnage, Frenzy finds the master of horror regaining his grip on the audience's pulse -- and making their blood run cold.

title: Ilsa, Harem Keeper of the Oil Sheiks
Abduction 2: Revenge of the Hive Queen
Monkey Man
rt_critics_consensus: An audacious effort from debuting director Dev Patel, Monkey Man dispenses action and sociopolitical commentary with equal aplomb.

title: Baskin
rt_critics_consensus: Baskin complements its gory thrills with heavy atmosphere and deliberate pacing, adding up to a horror outing that plays with the mind as enthusiastically as it ruins the appetite.

title: Attack of the Alien Jelly Monsters from the Depths of Uranus
Wrinkles the Clown
rt_critics_consensus: Wrinkles the Clown is an unsettling yet entertaining look at a bizarre modern myth that offers a uniquely thought-provoking, albeit slightly uneven, experience.

title: Possum
rt_critics_consensus: Unsettling and absorbing in equal measure, Possum presents a dark character study rich with rewards for fans of chilling genre fare.

title: Getaway
rt_critics_consensus: Monotonously fast-paced to the point of exhaustion, Getaway offers a reminder of the dangers in attempting to speed past coherent editing, character development, sensible dialogue, and an interesting plot.

title: The Iguana With the Tongue of Fire
Babyteeth
rt_critics_consensus: Powerfully acted and sensitively directed, Babyteeth offers audiences a coming-of-age story that's messier -- and more rewarding -- than most.

title: Creepy
rt_critics_consensus: Creepy lives up to its title with a suspenseful and thoroughly unsettling - not to mention well-acted - blend of crime procedural and domestic drama.

title: Halloween 5: The Revenge of Michael Myers
rt_critics_consensus: Halloween 5: The Revenge Of Michael Myers finds the series embracing crude slasher conventions with pedestrian scares, disposable characters, and aimless plotting.

title: The Torment of Laurie Ann Cullom
The Transfiguration
rt_critics_consensus: The Transfiguration tells a quieter, more deliberately paced tale than genre fans might expect, but for those with the patience to let it sink in, it offers its own rewards.

title: The Invitation
rt_critics_consensus: The Invitation makes brilliant use of its tension-rich premise to deliver a uniquely effective -- and surprisingly clever -- slow-building thriller.

title: Violation
rt_critics_consensus: Violation presents a powerful depiction of one woman's trauma -- and its uncomfortably gripping aftermath.

title: The Dead Thing
rt_critics_consensus: A potent metaphor for toxic relationships, The Dead Thing unfolds slowly but proves haunting thanks to its eerie mood and Blu Hunt's committed performance.

title: Beast of War
rt_critics_consensus: Honing its bite with sharp acting and compelling character dynamics, Beast of War utilizes historical events as good chum for sophisticated genre filmmaking.

title: Night of the Dead Sorority Babes
Skinwalkers
rt_critics_consensus: Skinwalkers is an atrociously-acted project whose unoriginal and ineptly-staged action sequences remind viewers of far better werewolf and action movies.

title: The Beasts
rt_critics_consensus: Rodrigo Sorogoyen throws us into rural Galicia with The Beasts, where tension and unease spread like wildfire in a scorching tale of decayed human nature.

title: Great White
rt_critics_consensus: It's far from the most toothless shark thriller, but Great White lacks the killer storytelling instinct necessary to stay afloat.

title: Beast
rt_critics_consensus: Beast plays like bleak poetry, unfurling its psychological thrills while guided by its captivating leads and mesmerizing, visceral visuals.

title: Yoga Hosers
rt_critics_consensus: Undisciplined, unfunny, and bereft of evident purpose, Yoga Hosers represents a particularly grating low point in Kevin Smith's once-promising career.

title: The Blackening
rt_critics_consensus: While it could stand to be a little funnier and quite a bit scarier, The Blackening is a thoughtful satire that skewers horror tropes and racial stereotypes.

title: The Red Queen Kills Seven Times
The Girl Who Wore Yellow Lace
Gerald's Game
rt_critics_consensus: Carla Gugino carries Gerald's Game's small-scale suspense with a career-defining performance.

title: Zombi Child
rt_critics_consensus: If the strain of its ambitious juggling act sometimes shows, Zombi Child remains an entertainingly audacious experience, enlivened with thought-provoking themes.

title: The Dark Side of the Moon
Hemet, or the Landlady Don't Drink Tea
Driven
rt_critics_consensus: Compelling performances from an outstanding cast help Driven take audiences on an enjoyably stranger-than-fiction joyride.

title: V/H/S/85
rt_critics_consensus: One of the franchise's stronger entries, V/H/S/85 largely avoids the quality control issues common to anthologies, with consistently creepy results.

title: The Messengers
rt_critics_consensus: The Messengers is an atmospheric but derivative rip-off of countless other horror movies.

title: The Legend of the Psychotic Forest Ranger
Threesome
rt_critics_consensus: Threesome's titillating title belies a dreadfully dull drama whose attractive stars are handily outmatched by a shallow script.

title: Happy Times
rt_critics_consensus: One of Zhang's smaller films, Happy Times is nevertheless moving and bittersweet.

title: Girl in Captivity 2: Hitwoman in Chains
Seed of Chucky
rt_critics_consensus: Give Seed of Chucky credit for embracing the increasing absurdity of the franchise -- even if the end results really aren't all that funny or entertaining.

title: Dead Walkers: Rise of the 4th Reich
Wild Things
rt_critics_consensus: Wild Things is a delightfully salacious, flesh-exposed romp that also requires a high degree of love for trash cinema.

title: Proof
rt_critics_consensus: Gwyneth Paltrow and Anthony Hopkins give exceptional performances in a film that intelligently tackles the territory between madness and genius.

title: Rec 4: Apocalypse
rt_critics_consensus: After a lackluster third entry, [REC] 4 gets the series back on track, at least to the level of the first sequel.

title: Devil's Night: Dawn of the Nain Rouge
Dawn of the Dead
rt_critics_consensus: A kinetic, violent and surprisingly worthy remake of George Romero's horror classic that pays homage to the original while working on its own terms.

title: Hell House LLC II: The Abaddon Hotel
Premonition
rt_critics_consensus: Overdosing on flashbacks, and more portentous than profound, the overly obtuse Premonition weakly echoes such twisty classics as Memento, The Sixth Sense, and Groundhog Day.

title: To Fire You Come at Last
Shark Night
rt_critics_consensus: A joyless excursion into the water that doesn't even produce good gore or nudity thanks to the neutered PG-13 rating.

title: Creepshow
rt_critics_consensus: It's uneven, as anthologies often are, but Creepshow is colorful, frequently funny, and treats its inspirations with infectious reverence.

title: Bats
rt_critics_consensus: Neither scary nor creepy.

title: You Shouldn't Have Let Me In
Hive
rt_critics_consensus: Anchored by Yllka Gashi's outstanding performance, Hive leads viewers on one woman's fact-based quest for self-determination in a patriarchal society.

title: The Boogeyman
rt_critics_consensus: The Boogeyman might fall short of its terrifying source material, but a spooky atmosphere and some solid performances help keep the chills coming.

title: Die Gstettensaga: The Rise of Echsenfriedl
The Girl Who Killed Her Parents
Case 39
rt_critics_consensus: Director Christian Alvert has a certain stylish flair, but it's wasted on Case 39's frightless, unoriginal plot.

title: Unspeakable: Beyond the Wall of Sleep
The Rule of Jenny Pen
rt_critics_consensus: John Lithgow's frighteningly unhinged performance reigns over The Rule of Jenny Pen, a nasty chiller that's by turns monotonous and haunting.

title: Sister Midnight
rt_critics_consensus: Mocking the pressures of matrimony with a sly wink, Sister Midnight is a thrilling genre-bender that's impossible to predict.

title: House on the Edge of the Park
The Last Thing Mary Saw
rt_critics_consensus: Its restrained approach may frustrate horror fans seeking more visceral thrills, but The Last Thing Mary Saw tells a haunting period tale.

title: Scary Stories to Tell in the Dark
rt_critics_consensus: Like the bestselling series of books that inspired it, Scary Stories to Tell in the Dark opens a creepy gateway into horror for younger genre enthusiasts.

title: Déjà Vu
rt_critics_consensus: Tony Scott tries to combine action, science fiction, romance, and explosions into one movie, but the time travel conceit might be too preposterous and the action falls apart under scrutiny.

title: Twin Peaks: Fire Walk With Me
rt_critics_consensus: For better or worse, Twin Peaks: Fire Walk With Me is every bit as strange and twisted as you'd expect from David Lynch.

title: Good Madam
rt_critics_consensus: Even if its ambitions are occasionally unwieldy, Good Madam offers effectively slow-burning horror anchored by thought-provoking themes.

title: The Golden Glove
rt_critics_consensus: Grim to a fault, The Golden Glove embarks on a well-crafted but deeply unsavory descent into the depraved mind and rank brutality of a serial killer.

title: For the Sake of Vicious
rt_critics_consensus: For the Sake of Vicious delivers on the gory promise of its title with a no-holds-barred thriller that should delight fans of pulpy B-movie splatter.

title: Boys from County Hell
rt_critics_consensus: Boys from County Hell stands out as an uncommonly good time in the crowded vampire genre -- and proves the Irish countryside is a fine setting for slaying the undead.

title: Van Helsing
rt_critics_consensus: A hollow creature feature that suffers from CGI overload.

title: The World Is Full of Secrets
Zoo
rt_critics_consensus: While a marginally fascinating look at a taboo subject, Zoo is bogged down by its overly artistic presentation.

title: Sleepover
rt_critics_consensus: 'Tween girls will enjoy this sugar coated fluff, but others will find Sleepover a snooze.

title: Fear Street: Prom Queen
rt_critics_consensus: Tired and uninspired, Fear Street plays like an '80s retread of better slashers, while ensuring the winning crown does not go to this Prom Queen.

title: Corporate Animals
rt_critics_consensus: Corporate culture may seem like easy pickings for satire, but the middling Corporate Animals proves even the broadest targets can be missed.

title: Last Night in Soho
rt_critics_consensus: Although it struggles to maintain its thrilling early momentum, Last Night in Soho shows flashes of Edgar Wright at his most stylish and ambitious.

title: Night of the Comet
rt_critics_consensus: Valley Girl culture satire Night of the Comet gets lots of mileage out of its slapstick sci-fi zombie approach.

title: Baghead
rt_critics_consensus: Pitting actors against murderers in a self-aware struggle for stardom, Baghead successfully skates the borders of horror and comedy.

title: We Have a Ghost
rt_critics_consensus: We Have a Ghost has a fun concept and a talented cast; unfortunately, this spookily uneven '80s pastiche also has very little idea of what to do with them.

title: Cleaver: Rise of the Killer Clown
Harry Knuckles and the Treasure of the Aztec Mummy
Blood Quantum
rt_critics_consensus: Blood Quantum blends bloody horror with sociopolitical subtext, taking a fresh bite out of the crowded zombie genre in the bargain.

title: Prince of Darkness
rt_critics_consensus: Prince of Darkness has a handful of chillingly clever ideas, but they aren't enough to put John Carpenter's return to horror at the same level as his classic earlier outings.

title: The One
rt_critics_consensus: The One plays more like a video game than a movie and borrows freely from other, better sci-fi actioners, burying Jet Li's spectacular talents under heaps of editing and special effects.

title: True Scary Story - Accident Property Entertainer 4
Zombieland
rt_critics_consensus: Wickedly funny and featuring plenty of gore, Zombieland is proof that the zombie subgenre is far from dead.

title: Bloody Hell
rt_critics_consensus: For genre fans in the mood to watch some darkly funny mayhem, Bloody Hell lives up to its title in all the best ways.

title: Don't Breathe 2
rt_critics_consensus: Stephen Lang remains a thrillingly imposing presence in Don't Breathe 2, but this sequel's story strains to find a sensible way forward for his character.

title: Cloud
rt_critics_consensus: Writer-director Kiyoshi Kurosawa returns to the theme of technology being a haunting force with disturbing success in Cloud, a slow-burn examination of social media that ends with a bang.

title: The Castle
rt_critics_consensus: Home is where the heart is in The Castle, a rousing Australian comedy that gives audiences an endearing family to root for.

title: More Than a Thousand Cameras Are Working for Your Safety
Henry: Portrait of a Serial Killer
rt_critics_consensus: Henry: Portrait of a Serial Killer is an effective, chilling profile of a killer that is sure to shock and disturb.

title: Snow White: A Tale of Terror
Surveillance
rt_critics_consensus: This dark psycho-thriller from Jennifer Lynch, is violent, sharp and baffling, but not to everyone's taste.

title: 28 Years Later
rt_critics_consensus: 28 Years Later taps into contemporary anxieties with the ferocious urgency of someone infected with Rage Virus, delivering a haunting and visceral thrill ride that defies expectations.

title: The Menu
rt_critics_consensus: While its social commentary relies on basic ingredients, The Menu serves up black comedy with plenty of flavor.

title: Amulet
rt_critics_consensus: If its chilly sense of dread never quite becomes spine-tingling terror, Amulet remains smart, solidly disquieting fun for genre fans seeking slow-building horror.

title: The Last Shift
rt_critics_consensus: The Last Shift doesn't quite reach the potential of its premise, however a powerful performance from Richard Jenkins makes it worth the watch.

title: Daddy's Head
rt_critics_consensus: Daddy's Head is a chilling and atmospheric slice of psychological horror, delivering unsettling imagery and a tense, unpredictable build-up.

title: Vampire Hunter D
rt_critics_consensus: Vampire Hunter D's gothic charms may be lost on those unfamiliar with the anime series that spawned it, but the crisp action and nightmarish style will satiate horror aficionados' bloodlust.

title: The Wisdom of Crocodiles
rt_critics_consensus: Stylish, but emotionally uninvolving.

title: That Cold Dead Look in Your Eyes
Stuck
rt_critics_consensus: Steeped in gallows humor, Stuck is a taut, tense examination of a tragic accident.

title: The Endless
rt_critics_consensus: The Endless benefits from its grounded approach to an increasingly bizarre story, elevated by believable performances by filmmakers Justin Benson and Aaron Moorhead.

title: Prema Entha Madhuram Priyuraalu Antha Katinam
A Quiet Place Part II
rt_critics_consensus: A nerve-wracking continuation of its predecessor, A Quiet Place Part II expands the terrifying world of the franchise without losing track of its heart.

title: Resident Evil: The Final Chapter
rt_critics_consensus: Resident Evil: The Final Chapter may prove mind-numbingly chaotic for the unconverted, but for fans of the venerable franchise, it offers a fittingly kinetic conclusion to its violent post-apocalyptic saga.

title: Dreamscape
rt_critics_consensus: Dreamscape mixes several genres -- horror, sci-fi, action -- and always maintains a sense of adventure and humor.

title: Eight Legged Freaks
rt_critics_consensus: This homage to the B-movies of the '50s has a promising first half, but runs out of ideas in the second.

title: Lost in Translation
rt_critics_consensus: Effectively balancing humor and subtle pathos, Sofia Coppola crafts a moving, melancholy story that serves as a showcase for both Bill Murray and Scarlett Johansson.

title: Sound of Violence
rt_critics_consensus: The consistency isn't always smooth, but Sound of Violence's blend of character study and horror marks writer-director Alex Noyer as an ambitious, visually distinctive filmmaker.

title: The Abyss
rt_critics_consensus: The utterly gorgeous special effects frequently overshadow the fact that The Abyss is also a totally gripping, claustrophobic thriller, complete with an interesting crew of characters.

title: My Bloody Valentine
rt_critics_consensus: My Bloody Valentine stumbles through a cinematic coal mine of mechanical scares, bland characters, and a contrived, blood-soaked plot that's as predictable as it is unimaginative.

title: 12 Hour Shift
rt_critics_consensus: Sharp, twisted, and darkly funny, 12 Hour Shift is an uncommonly clever heist caper with a refreshing female-focused twist.

title: Horror Noire: A History of Black Horror
rt_critics_consensus: Horror Noire: A History of Black Horror more than lives up to its title, offering a smart and entertaining overview of American film history through an overlooked lens.

title: Thr3e
rt_critics_consensus: Thr3e is a shoddily made, thrill-free thriller that isn't half as good as the several movies it borrows from (Adaptation, Saw, Se7en).

title: Oculus
rt_critics_consensus: With an emphasis on dread over gore and an ending that leaves the door wide open for sequels, Oculus could be just the first spine-tingling chapter in a new franchise for discerning horror fans.

title: Nightmare Cinema
rt_critics_consensus: Admirably eclectic yet more consistent than most horror anthologies, Nightmare Cinema should entertain viewers in the mood for a good old-fashioned creepshow.

title: Candyman
rt_critics_consensus: Though it ultimately sacrifices some mystery in the name of gory thrills, Candyman is a nuanced, effectively chilling tale that benefits from an interesting premise and some fine performances.

title: Unicorn Wars
rt_critics_consensus: While its humor may strike some as needlessly crude, Unicorn Wars is a visually audacious strike against cultural and military fascism.

title: My Animal
rt_critics_consensus: Jacqueline Castel's feature debut is an arresting queer and lycanthropic coming of age story that longs for more, but delivers just enough panache to satisfy.

title: Ghosts in the Graveyard: Pure Pandemonium
The Night Evelyn Came Out of the Grave
They/Them
rt_critics_consensus: Although it deserves credit for its strong cast and inclusive premise, They/Them is too tonally messy to cut more than skin deep.

title: Dare
rt_critics_consensus: Dare's sharp writing and appealing young cast are often enough to outweigh its jarringly jumbled narrative.

title: Scream 7
rt_critics_consensus: Less a return to Scream's roots than a disappointing creative regression, this seventh entry draws little blood with its dull knife of a script.

title: Strange Harvest
rt_critics_consensus: Getting the little details of true crime documentaries right before unleashing some truly unsettling twists, Strange Harvest reaps a bountiful crop of scares from its inventive structure.

title: The King Kong That Appeared in Edo
Resident Evil: Apocalypse
rt_critics_consensus: Resident Evil: Apocalypse has lots of action, but not much in terms of plot or creativity.

title: Rubber
rt_critics_consensus: A clever premise gets plenty of comic blood and violence, but its hampered by some questionable storytelling techniques from director Quentin Dupieux.

title: What Ever Happened to Baby Jane?
rt_critics_consensus: What Ever Happened to Baby Jane? combines powerhouse acting, rich atmosphere, and absorbing melodrama in service of a taut thriller with thought-provoking subtext.

title: Dragon Ball: Sleeping Princess in Devil's Castle
Dead Squad: Temple of the Undead
Uzumaki
rt_critics_consensus: Uzumaki uses its creepy, David Lynch-inspired atmospherics to effectively build a sense of dread, but ultimately fails to do anything with it.

title: Stevie
